_meta.attribution: Data from the Corpus Coranicum Project (BBAW), 2007-2024. TEI export by Yasmin Faghihi and Huw Jones (Cambridge); technical implementation by Claus Franke and Marcus Lampert (BBAW - TELOTA).
_meta.filterNote: Filtered to manuscripts with a parseable date center <= 1100 CE so the timeline view stays readable. The full 2323-entry catalogue lives upstream at https://github.com/telota/corpus-coranicum-tei/tree/main/data/quran_manuscripts.
_meta.featuredFilter: featured = imageUrls.length > 0 AND parseable date <= 1000 CE. ManuscriptTimeline.jsx uses this subset to keep the visualisation readable.
_meta.famousFallbackPolicy: For codices the manuscript-studies literature consistently cites but whose CC TEI msDesc has no image URLs (Sanaa palimpsest fragments, BnF Arabe 328 series, Birmingham Mingana 1572a, Tübingen Ma VI 165), we attach a stable Wikimedia Commons thumbnail or institutional viewer URL so they appear in the featured set. Affected entries carry featuredFallback: true. No image content is fabricated; the URLs point at public, third-party-hosted images of these specific manuscripts.

manuscripts.summary: Das im ḥiǧāzī -Schriftstil verfasste, 30 sehr beschädigte Blätter umfassende Fragment gehört zu den Handschriften, die 1971/72 bei Renovierungsarbeiten der Großen Moschee von Sanaa entdeckt wurden. Nach einem Abkommen zwischen dem Jemen und der Bundesrepublik Deutschland, wurden die Koranfragmente im Rahmen eines Kulturerhaltungsprojektes zur Restaurierung und Katalogisierung der Handschriften bearbeitet, das von Albrecht Noth (Hamburg) initiiert worden war und von Gerd-Rüdiger Puin (Saarbrücken) 1980-1984/85, Hans-Caspar Graf von Bothmer (Saarbrücken) 1984-1986 und Ursula Dreibholz (Sanaa)…

manuscripts.title: Berlin, Staatsbibliothek zu Berlin, Ms. or. fol. 4313 (VOHD 17, A, 1, 1)
manuscripts.repository: Staatsbibliothek zu Berlin , Berlin
manuscripts.summary: 7 fol. on parchment; Shelfmark: Ms. or. fol. 4313 (VOHD 17, A, 1, 1); 18 lines per page; Dimensions: 393 x 262mm; Script style: ḥiǧāzī (close to type II); Estimated date of production: before 680 (?).
manuscripts.script: The manuscript is written in a variation of ḥiǧāzī type II according to the typology of François Déroche ( Déroche, 1983 ). The main feature of this script style is the letter ʾalif without the return at the base line. In all other ḥiǧāzī script styles the letter has a smal return at the base on the right. The important example of this script style is the Qur'an manuscript Or. 2165 from the British Library in London. Other known example of this script style is the manuscript of the Qur'an Arabe 328 (e) from the Bibliotèque nationale de France in Paris. The main characteristic of this script style is also that the vertical features of the script such as ʾalif, lām, vertical stroke of ṭāʾ/ẓāʾ are long and leaning generously to the right. Interesting feature of the manuscript ms.or.fol. 4313 is the writing of the letter ǧīm in the final or isolated position. The letter ends in a curve that is open to the right, unlike the shape seen in the manuscripts Arabe 328 (e) and Or. 2165 where the tail of the letter lies horizontally on the base lina and ends in a very small hook. The letter ʿayn in the final position shows a slightly different writing as well. The tail of the letter is slightly more defined than in the other two manuscripts mentioned above where the letter ends in a vertical stroke with a minimal hook. The ending of the letter qāf in the final od isolated position is also round with a bigger curve than in other two manuscripts in comparison. The letter in Arabe 328 (e) and Or. 2165 has a vertical tail that ends in a more angular hook. The script of the Berlin fragment is in general rounder and curvier than those of Arabe 328 (e) and Or. 2165 , which can also be observed in the shape of the letter nūn when isolated or in final position. The letter m īm in the final of isolated position has a small horizontal tail, whereas in other two ḥiǧāzī II fragments the letter ends in a small vertical stroke. Finally, the letter y ā ʾ in the final position is always written reversed in Arabe 328 (e) and Or. 2165 , the feature, that is not attested in the Berlin fragment. The manuscript shows occasional diacritical marking. The vocalisation is missing. Typical letter shapes are presented in the palaeographic table, accessible as image no. 2 under fol. 1r. (Salome Beridze)

manuscripts.title: Cairo, Khedivial Library, today: Egyptian National Library and Archives (دار الكتب والوثائق القومية), Moritz 1905, table 44
manuscripts.repository: Khedivial Library, today: Egyptian National Library and Archives (دار الكتب والوثائق القومية) , Cairo
manuscripts.summary: Das Bild der Seite stammt aus dem Album Arabic Palaeography (Kairo 1905) von Bernhard Moritz (1859-1939), der von 1896 bis 1911 Direktor der Bibliothek des Khediven in Kairo war. Die Aufnahme belegt, dass die im Gotthelf-Bersträßer-Photoarchiv befindlichen Fotos einer Handschrift mit der Signatur qāf 47 ( ق ٤٧ ) aus Kairo stammen, da sich unter ihnen ein Foto derselben Seite befindet. Sieben weitere Blätter mit der Signatur ms.or.fol. 4313 , die im September 1939 von der Königlich Preußischen Bibliothek (heute Staatsbibliothek zu Berlin) erworben wurden, stammen ursprünglich aus demselben…

manuscripts.title: Tübingen, Universitätsbibliothek Tübingen, Ma VI 165
manuscripts.repository: Universitätsbibliothek Tübingen , Tübingen
manuscripts.summary: 77 fol. on parchment, Shelfmark: Ma VI 165; Qurʾānic Text: Q 17:35- Q 36:57; 18-21 lines per page; Dimensions: 195 x 153mm; Script style: kūfī, type B.Ia; Estimated date of production: 650-700 (?).
manuscripts.script: The script of this Qur'an manuscript represents the early kūfī script style B.Ia according to the palaeography classification of F. Déroche (Déroche 1992: PN 38-39) . The most important feature of this script group is that the shaft of the letter ʾalif and other vertical characters slope to the right as in ḥiǧāzī . This characteristic is present in the manuscript. The letter ǧīm in the medial form has a horizontal appearance which differs from the vertical bar of the letter placed across the base line of the B.I group. In the final position the letter form is similar to the early kūfī group. At the end of a word the letter ʿayn is similar to those of ḥiǧāzī IV style. When isolated, the letter shows the same form of the tail as ǧīm . The body of the letter nūn in the B.Ia goup is rounder than the one in the Tübingen manuscript. In this case the letter is similar to ḥiǧāzī IV or B.Ib. The body of the letter q āf is leaning to the right as in ḥiǧāzī scripts, whereas the B.I group shows a vertical appearance of the letter. In all other respects the script is similar to the B.Ia group. The manuscript shows occasional diacritical marking. The vocalisation is made by red dots. A later hand in black ink uses the modern vocalisation system in the manuscript. Typical letter shapes are presented in the palaeographic table, accessible as image no. 4 under fol. 1r. (Salome Beridze)

manuscripts.title: Rome, Biblioteca Apostolica Vaticana, Vat. ar. 1785
manuscripts.repository: Biblioteca Apostolica Vaticana , Rome
manuscripts.summary: 9 fol. on parchment; Shelfmark Vat. ar. 1785; Qurʾānic Text: Q 18:30-19:81, Q 19:83-20:116; 20 lines per page; Script style close to early kūfī type B.Ia with elements of ḥiǧāzī IV according to the palaeographical classification of F. Déroche from 1992 . Estimated date of production: 650-700 AD.
manuscripts.script: The script of this Qurʾān manuscript is close to the early kūfī script style B.Ia or ḥiǧāzī IV (according to the typologiy of François Déroche ). The shaft of ʾalif and other vertical upstrokes slant to the right, that is a characteristic of B.Ia and ḥiǧāzī scripts. The letter ǧīm in the initial position is closer to the B.I group. When in the middle of a word, the letter shaped like a bar that crosses the base line. This form is found in ḥiǧāzī IV as well as in the B.Ia group. The final form of the letter has a horizontal tail that end in a small hook, as in ḥiǧāzī IV and B.I manuscripts. The upper stroke of the letter ṭāʾ slopes to the right as in ḥiǧāzī and B.Ia. The letter ʿayn when isolated has the same tail with a small hook as the letter ǧīm . Q āf in the final position or isolated is more elongated vertically than in B.I manuscripts. The letter is vertical and does not slant to right as in ḥiǧāzī . The letter nūn is more similar to ḥiǧāzī IV with its vertical body with a short return. The initial and medial hāʾ rests on the base line and does not extend below it. This characteristic is closer to the ḥiǧāzī group. The letter m īm in the middle and at the end of a word also rests on the base line, as in ḥiǧāzī scripts or kūfī A.I script style. The script of the fragment could be considered as a transitional stage between the ḥiǧāzī and early kūfī scripts. The diacritical marks are in the shape of small dashes. No trace of vocalisation (red dots) can be observed, which could be another indication for the early dating of the fragment. Small strokes placed at the end of a line serve as line fillers.

manuscripts.title: Berlin, Staatsbibliothek zu Berlin, Wetzstein II 1913 (Ahlwardt 305)
manuscripts.repository: Staatsbibliothek zu Berlin , Berlin
manuscripts.summary: 210 fol. on parchment; Shelfmark: Wetzstein II 1913; Qurʾānic text: Q 2:30-21:112, Q 22:18-24:33, Q 24:51-27:84, Q 34:47-74:1, Q 78:35-90:18; 20-23 lines per page; Dimensions: 340 x 270mm; Script style: kūfī type B.Ia; Estimated date of production: before 710 (?).
manuscripts.script: The text of this manuscript is written in the early kūfī script style B.Ia ( F. Déroche, 1992 ). The ʾalif slightly slopes to the right, as well as other vertical shafts of the script, such as the letter ṭāʾ/ẓāʾ . The initial letter ʿayn with the vertical right antenna is close to the B.Ib style (which is very close to the B.Ia style), with the difference that the two antennas are hardly differentiable. The letter qāf isolated or in the final position has vertical appearance and a pronounced tail similar to the B.I group. The shape of the letter nūn is closer to those of B.Ib with the more vertical body and the angular lower return. The diacritical marks are in shape of oblique dots. The letter qāf is marked with a dot below the letter. The diacritical dot of the letter ḏāl is placed on the left side of the letter, that is after the letter. The vocalisation is made by red dots. Typical letter shapes are presented in the palaeographic table, accessible as image no. 4 under fol. 1r. (Salome Beridze). Measurement results of carbon dating are given as images no. 2 and 3 under fol. 1r. (Salome Beridze)

manuscripts.title: Leiden, Universiteitsbibliotheek Leiden, Cod.or. 14.545a
manuscripts.repository: Universiteitsbibliotheek Leiden , Leiden
manuscripts.summary: 4 parchment folios; Shelfmark: Cod.or. 14.545a; Qurʾānic Text: Q 2:269-275, Q 2:277-282, Q 17:40-110; 20 lines per page; Dimensions: [undamaged folio: ca. 505 x 330mm, dimensions of Arabe 335 ]; Script style: kūfī, type B.Ib; Estimated date of production: 660-710 (??).
manuscripts.script: This manuscript represents the early kūfī script style B.Ib. The main difference between the styles B.Ia and B.Ib is that in case of the B.Ia group the shaft of ʾalif and other vertical strokes slope to the right ( See Déroche, 1992 ). The letter ʾalif in the manuscript is vertical. As for the letter group ǧīm/ḥāʾ/ḫāʾ in the isolated position, they show resemblance to the script style ḥiǧāzī IV as the horizontal tail is alongated and ends in a short hook. The final letter ʿayn also shares features with the ḥiǧāzī IV group, the body of the letter is more pronounced and angular, than in B.I. In the initial position, the letter shows a round upper curve closer to the B.II group. The initial ʿayn of the B.I group has a short angular hook. The same is the case for the letter in the medial position: the two antennas are hardly distinguishable, the right hand antenna being vertical, as in B.II. The qāf isolated or in the final position shows again a different form, the body is more cursive and less vertical than in B.I. Oblique strokes are marking the diacritica of the script. The qāf is marked by a dot below the letter, as in most early kūfī Qur'an manuscripts. The vocalisation is done by red dots. Typical letter shapes are presented in the palaeographic table, accessible as image no. 2 under fol. 1r. (Salome Beridze)

manuscripts.title: Leiden, Universiteitsbibliotheek Leiden, Cod.or. 14.545b
manuscripts.repository: Universiteitsbibliotheek Leiden , Leiden
manuscripts.script: Script style of the fragment Cod.or. 14.545b is the early kūfī type B.Ia according to the palaeographical classification of F. Déroche from 1992 . The vertical shaft of the letter ʾalif shows the characteristic of leaning to the right as well as the shaft of the letter ṭāʾ/ẓāʾ . The script shows hardly any variations from the script group B.I described by F. Déroche. The final ǧīm/ḥāʾ/ḫāʾ of in isolated position has a hook which is round and more pronounced than in the B.I group. The letter ʿayn when isolated shows the same round body of the letter and differs from the same letter in the final position. The diacritical marks are indicated by small oblique dots. The vocalisation is missing. Ma šq is used in the script, which is the elongation of the horizontal components of the script. (Salome Beridze)

manuscripts.title: Doha, Qatar National Library (مكتبة قطرالوطنية), HC.MS.2016.0015
manuscripts.repository: Qatar National Library (مكتبة قطرالوطنية) , Doha
manuscripts.summary: Single folio; Shelfmark: HC.MS.2016.0015; Qurʾānic Text: 038:076-039:021; Lines per page: 21; Dimensions: 332 x 222 mm; Script stye: ḥiǧāzī; Ductus: ḥiǧāzī with similarities to ḥiǧāzī I and ḥiǧāzī III; Estimated date of production: 650-720.
manuscripts.script: Ductus: ḥiǧāzī , with similarities to ḥiǧāzī I and ḥiǧāzī III Diacritics: There is partial use of letter-pointing, but no vowel signs. Letter-pointing is marked with brown ink dots of the same ink as the text and of the same shape as the nib-end of the calamus. The two dots indicating the tā’ are positioned vertically one above the other over the denticle. The three dots marking the ṯā’ are positioned above the denticle in a triangular cluster. The three dots indicating the šīn are placed one above each denticle in a horizontal row. Discussion of the script: The script has features found in scripts originally designated as ḥiǧāzī I, ḥiǧāzī II and ḥiǧāzī III ( Déroche 1983, pp. 35-6 ). Overall the script is closest to that used on a fragment in the Oriental Institute Chicago A 7001 , although on the present leaf and sister leaves the script is slightly more spaciously arranged and the letters slant slightly more to the right. Individual diagnostic letter forms in the script found on this leaf and on sister leaves from the same manuscript are as follows: The majority of examples of the independent ʾalif have a slight curve in the ascender and slope somewhat to the right. Some examples of the letter form curve to the right more emphatically at the base, giving those examples a short stub-like return. Other examples have a slightly straighter ascender with no lower return. In general the ascenders of the ʾalif and other letters with vertical elements are quite short. When ʾalif is followed immediately by lām , the two ascenders most often slant or curve away from each other, slightly diverging. The medial form ǧīm/ḥāʾ/ḫāʾ has an oblique stroke that drops below the line, as is typical of ḥiǧāzī scripts. Final ǧīm/ḥā’/ḫāʾ has a tail that is shaped in a fairly regular curve like a sickle, a form very similar to that found on A 7001 in the Oriental Institute (Chicago) , and similar to some examples found on ms.or.fol.4313 in the Staatsbibliothek Berlin and Chester Beatty Library Ms.1615 II . Dāl/ḏāl varies slightly in its exact form, but all variations are found on other Qur’āns in ḥiǧāzī scripts as well as in scripts found on other media in the second half of the 7th century and the earliest years of the 8th century (see Fraser 2012, p. 129 ). The ascender of the ṭā’/ẓā’ slants slightly to the right and is similar to the form found on British Library manuscript Or. 2165 and BNF fragment Arabe 328e . Most examples of final ‘ayn and ġayn are similar to Hand B of the Codex Parisino-petropolitanus ( Déroche 2014, fig. 2 ) and BNF Arabe 328e . Final fā’/qāf has a tail with a short vertical element immediately below the line which then curves into an open sickle-shaped hook, very similar to the forms found in ms.or.fol.4313 in the Staatsbibliothek Berlin and Chester Beatty Library Ms.1615 II . In the case of the final or independent kāf , the lower horizontal line extends considerably further to the left than the upper horizontal line, a useful diagnostic for ḥiǧāzī scripts ( see Déroche 1999 ). The form found on the present script is similar to that in the British Library manuscript Or. 2165 and BNF fragment Arabe 328e and those in Hands C and E of the Codex Parisino-petropolitanus ( Déroche 2014, figs. 3, 5 ), although the ascender here is slightly more vertical and the gap between the lower and upper horizontals is slightly more open. The lām-ʾalif has a fairly regular form with two diverging ascenders. The ascender that rises to the right is mostly straight, whereas the ascender that rises to the left is more often slightly curved. It resembles a slightly more rounded version of the lām-ʾalif found in Oriental Institute Chicago A 7001 . Final mīm has two forms: one has a short horizontal tail, some of which curve upwards slightly; the other has a short pointed stub that points downwards. Both forms are found frequently in many examples of ḥiǧāzī script. Final nūn has an sickle-shaped form and is very similar to that found on A 7001 in the Oriental Institute, Chicago , and is also close to examples found on ms.or.fol.4313 in the Staatsbibliothek Berlin . The initial and medial form of hā’ has a distinctive upper diagonal tail that rises from the top and slants to the left. The form is very close to that found in the Chester Beatty Library Ms.1615 I and Chicago Oriental Institute A 7001 , and not dissimilar to the form in British Library Ms. Or. 2165 and BNF fragment Arabe 328e , although the nib of the calamus here is slimmer. The final yā’ has a rounded hook shape very close to that found in Chicago Oriental Institute A 7001 and Berlin ms.or.fol.4313 .

manuscripts.title: Chicago, Institute for the Study of Ancient Cultures, A 6959
manuscripts.repository: Institute for the Study of Ancient Cultures , Chicago
manuscripts.summary: Single parchment folio; Shelfmark: A 6959; Qurʾānic text: Q 68:9-68:24, Q 68:36-68:45; Originally 18 lines per page; Script style: kūfī type B.Ia.
manuscripts.script: Script style of the fragment A 6959 is the early kūfī type B.Ia according to the palaeographical classification of F. Déroche from 1992 . The vertical shaft of the letter ʾalif shows the characteristic of leaning to the right as well as the shaft of the letter ṭāʾ/ẓāʾ . The script shows hardly any variations from the script group B.I described by F. Déroche (Déroche 1992: PN 38-39) . The final ǧīm/ḥāʾ/ḫāʾ in isolated position has a hook which is round and more pronounced than in the B.I group. The letter ʿayn when isolated shows the same round body of theletter and differs from the same letter in the final position. The diacritical marks are indicated by small oblique dots. The vocalisation is missing. Ma šq is used in the script, which is the elongation of the horizontal components of the script. (Salome Beridze)

manuscripts.title: Paris, Bibliothèque nationale de France, Arabe 335
manuscripts.repository: Bibliothèque nationale de France , Paris
manuscripts.summary: Qur'an fragment on parchment with 10 folios; Shelfmark Arabe 335; 20 lines per page; script style: kūfī B.Ib.
manuscripts.script: This manuscript represents the early kūfī script style B.Ib. The main difference between the styles B.Ia and B.Ib is that in case of the B.Ia group the shaft of ʾalif and other vertical strokes slope to the right ( See Déroche, 1992 ). The letter ʾalif in the manuscript is vertical. As for the letter group ǧīm/ḥāʾ/ḫāʾ in the isolated position, they show resemblance to the script style ḥiǧāzī IV as the horizontal tail is alongated and ends in a short hook. The final letter ʿayn also shares features with the ḥiǧāzī IV group, the body of the letter is more pronounced and angular, than in B.I. In the initial position, the letter shows a round upper curve closer to the B.II group. The initial ʿayn of the B.I group has a short angular hook. The same is the case for the letter in the medial position: the two antennas are hardly distinguishable, the right hand antenna being vertical, as in B.II. The qāf isolated or in the final position shows again a different form, the body is more cursive and less vertical than in B.I. Oblique strokes are marking the diacritica of the script. The qāf is marked by a dot below the letter, as in most early kūfī Qur'an manuscripts. The vocalisation is done by red dots. (Salome Beridze)

manuscripts.script: The script style of this Qurʾān manuscript is the early kūfī B.Ib (Déroche 1992: PN 38-39) . The shaft of the letter ʾalif is vertical; the isolated form has a horizontal return at the baseline. The letter group ǧīm/ḥāʾ/ḫāʾ in the independent and final form has a small horizontal tail which ends with a short hook. The ʿayn in the final form shows a different form of its tail, which has a longer return than in other B.Ib copies. The original diacritical marks are indicated by small dashes. There seems to be a second layer of the diacritical marking with small dots. (Salome Beridze)

The letter ʾalif in the manuscript is vertical. As for the letter group ǧīm/ḥāʾ/ḫāʾ in the isolated position, they show resemblance to the script style ḥiǧāzī IV as the horizontal tail is alongated and ends in a short hook. The final letter ʿayn also shares features with the ḥiǧāzī IV group, the body of the letter is more pronounced and angular, than in B.I. In the initial position, the letter shows a round upper curve closer to the B.II group. The initial ʿayn of the B.I group has a short angular hook. The same is the case for the letter in the medial position: the two antennas are hardly distinguishable, the right hand antenna being vertical, as in B.II. The qāf isolated or in the final position shows again a different form, the body is more cursive and less vertical than in B.I. Oblique strokes are marking the diacritica of the script. The qāf is marked by a dot below the letter, as in most early kūfī Qur'an manuscripts. The vocalisation is done by red dots. (Salome Beridze)

manuscripts.title: London, The Khalili Collections, KFQ 50
manuscripts.repository: The Khalili Collections , London
manuscripts.summary: This fragment originally belonged to the same codex from al-Fusṭāṭ (Old-Cairo) as six other sister fragments: Arabe 335 (Paris), Cod. Or. 14.545 a (Leiden), A 6958 (Chicago), Marcel 5 (St. Petersburg), E 16264 K (University of Pennsylvania Museum Philadelphia) and MS.276 (Museum of Islamic Art, Doḥa, Qatar). The radiocarbon analysis carried out on the Leiden Frgment Cod. Or. 14.545 a dates the codex between 652-763 AD (with the probability of 95,4%).
manuscripts.script: This manuscript represents the early kūfī script style B.Ib. The main difference between the styles B.Ia and B.Ib is that in case of the B.Ia group the shaft of ʾalif and other vertical strokes slope to the right ( See Déroche, 1992 ). The letter ʾalif in the manuscript is vertical. As for the letter group ǧīm/ḥāʾ/ḫāʾ in the isolated position, they show resemblance to the script style ḥiǧāzī IV as the horizontal tail is alongated and ends in a short hook. The final letter ʿayn also shares features with the ḥiǧāzī IV group, the body of the letter is more pronounced and angular, than in B.I. In the initial position, the letter shows a round upper curve closer to the B.II group. The initial ʿayn of the B.I group has a short angular hook. The same is the case for the letter in the medial position: the two antennas are hardly distinguishable, the right hand antenna being vertical, as in B.II. The qāf isolated or in the final position shows again a different form, the body is more cursive and less vertical than in B.I. Oblique strokes are marking the diacritica of the script. The qāf is marked by a dot below the letter, as in most early kūfī Qur'an manuscripts. The vocalisation is done by red dots. (Salome Beridze).

manuscripts.title: London, Sotheby’s Auction House, Arts Of The Islamic World, 20 April 2016, Lot 10
manuscripts.shelfmark: Arts Of The Islamic World, 20 April 2016, Lot 10
manuscripts.repository: Sotheby’s Auction House , London
manuscripts.summary: Single leaf Qur'an manuscript on parchment; London, Ms. Sotheby's, 20. April 2016, Lot 10; 18 lines to the page; Ex-private collection, France, sold to Sotheby's on 20. April, 2016; Script style: early kūfī type B.I.
manuscripts.script: The script of the fragment is close to the kūfī style B.I with the influence of ḥiǧāzī and kūfī C.Ia (Déroche 1992) . The letter ʾalif is slanting to the right slightly, as in ḥiǧāzī and kūfī B.Ia scripts. The letters ǧīm/ḥāʾ/ḫāʾ in the initial position resembles the letter seen in the C.Ia group. Within a word, the letter rests on the base line as those in C.Ia. In one case on the fol. 1r. of the sister fragment from Millon, Paris the letter in the medial position has a form of an oblique bar that crosses the base line, like in the B.I group. The final form of the letter is also identical to the C.Ia group, where the tail of the letter is horizontal. When initial, the letter ʿayn is close to those of ḥiǧāzī IV with the upper hook being angular rather than round. In the medial form the letter is identical to those of B.II group, the two antennas are difficult to distinguish, but the right one is vertical. In the final position the letter shows two different shapes: when isolated, the tail lies horizontally on the base line . When connected to the previous letter, the form resembles those of B.Ia. The qāf in final position is identical to B.II. On the fol. 1r. of the sister fragment from Millon, Paris the isolated qāf shows resemblance to the B.I group. The letter hāʾ in the initial or medial position rests on the base line, the bar is vertical, resembling the form seen in ḥiǧāzī IV manuscripts. As for lām-ʾalif, the two arms of the ligature curve away from each other, other than in B.I where they are leaning towards each other. The vocalisation is made by red dots. The diacritical marking happens occasionally and is made by oblique dashes. Typical letter shapes are presented in the palaeographic table, accessible as image no. 2 under fol. 1r. (Salome Beridze)

manuscripts.script: Script style of this fragment is the early kūfī type B.Ia according to the palaeographical classification of F. Déroche from 1992 . The vertical shaft of the letter ʾalif shows the characteristic of leaning to the right as well as the shaft of the letter ṭāʾ/ẓāʾ . The script shows hardly any variations from the script group B.I described by F. Déroche. The final ǧīm/ḥāʾ/ḫāʾ in isolated position has a hook which is round and more pronounced than in the B.I group. The letter ʿayn when isolated shows the same round body of theletter and differs from the same letter in the final position. The diacritical marks are indicated by small oblique dots. The vocalisation is missing. Ma šq is used in the script, which is the elongation of the horizontal components of the script. (Salome Beridze)

manuscripts.summary: 1 folio on parchment; Qurʾānic Text: Q 87:19-90:20; 18 lines per page; Dimensions: 585 x 425mm; Script style: kūfī B.I; Estimated date of production: 680-750 (?).
manuscripts.script: The script of the fragment is close to the kūfī style B.I with influences from ḥiǧāzī and C.Ia. The letter ʾalif is slightly inclined to the right, as in the ḥiǧāzī and B.Ia scripts. The letters ǧīm/ḥāʾ/ḫāʾ in initial position resemble the letter found in the C.Ia group. Within a word, the letter rests on the base line as in C.Ia. In one case on fol. 1r. the letter in the medial position has the form of an oblique bar crossing the base line, as in the B.I group. The final form of the letter is also identical to group C.Ia, where the tail of the letter is horizontal. In the initial form, the letter ʿayn is close to that of ḥiǧāzī IV, with the upper hook being angular rather than round. In the medial form, the letter is identical to those of the B.II group, the two antennas are difficult to distinguish, but the right one is vertical. In the final position, the letter has two different shapes: when isolated, the tail lies horizontally on the base line. When connected to the previous letter, the shape is similar to that of B.Ia. The letter qāf in final position is identical to B.II. On fol. 1r. the isolated qāf bears a resemblance to the B.I group. The letter hāʾ in the initial or medial position rests on the baseline, the bar is vertical, resembling the form seen in the ḥiǧāzī IV manuscripts. As for lām-ʾalif ligature, the two arms of the ligature curve away from each other, unlike in B.I. where they lean towards each other. Vocalisation is done with red dots. Diacritical marks are occasionally made by oblique strokes. Typical letter forms are shown in the palaeographic table, accessible as fig. 2 under fol. 1r.

manuscripts.title: Doha, Qatar National Library (مكتبة قطرالوطنية), HC.MS.03222
manuscripts.repository: Qatar National Library (مكتبة قطرالوطنية) , Doha
manuscripts.summary: Single folio; Shelfmark: HC.MS.03222; Text: 005:029-005:044; Lines per page: 22; Dimensions: 412 x 299 mm; Script stye: B.I/late ḥiǧāzī; Ductus: Close to B.Ia with ḥiǧāzī influence; Estimated date of production: 690-750.
manuscripts.script: Diacritics: Letter-pointing consists of small brown ink dots the shape of the end of the nib of the calamus. There is no vocalisation. Discussion of the script: The present script is a transitional script between ḥiǧāzī and the kūfī B scripts. It could be characterised as either a late ḥiǧāzī script in which characteristics of B scripts are strongly present, or an early B script in which the influence of ḥiǧāzī scripts is still present. The evidence of individual diagnostic letter forms discussed below underlines this transitional status. The thickness of the strokes is also close to B scripts. Déroche describes the B scripts as overlapping with ḥiǧāzī scripts, especially B.Ia ( Déroche 1983, pp. 37-8 ), and the present script is a good example of this transitional nature. The regularity of the forms of the various examples of the same letter on the page and the relatively strict line discipline and text frame discipline indicates a phase of professionalism and codification of scribal practices. The closest comparison to the script overall is found on Sanaa DAM 00-28.1 and it may be that the present folio is from the same parent manuscript, but this is not yet certain, as only one page from DAM 00-28.1 has so far been published ( Catalogue Maṣāḥif Sanʿāʾ, p. 56 ). The script on the page of Sana‘a DAM 01-28.1 illustrated in the catalogue Maṣāḥif Ṣanʿāʾ is certainly similar to the present script, the dimensions of the folios are the same, they have the same number of lines per page, and the tenth verse markers are similar. However, there are also differences in several letter forms, such as the ʾalif , final fā’/qāf , final nūn and final yā’ , and in the single verse separators. It is possible that the present leaf and Sanaa DAM 00-28.1 were originally part of the same manuscript but were written by different scribes at a time when scribal professionalism and consistency of letter forms was becoming established and there was therefore a closer affinity between the hands of different scribes (see Déroche 2014, pp. 100 for a related discussion). The comment in Masahif Sana‘a on DAM 00-28.1 states “The leaves show noticeable stylistic variations and a tendency towards a calligraphic appearance” an observation that would correspond with the present leaf. Individual diagnostic letter forms in the script on this leaf are as follows: The initial ʾalif has a form that lies between ḥiǧāzī and B.Ia. It has a long return at the base and most examples slope slightly to the right. Certain examples of ḥiǧāzī script feature a related form, such as the script on BNF Arabe 328d and Sanaa DAM 00.29.1 ( Maṣāḥif Ṣanʿāʾ, p. 58 ), as well as some examples of ḥiǧāzī III and IV ( Déroche 1983, pls. VI-VII ). However, the form here is slimmer and has a more angular bend at the base than those found in the script designated as ḥiǧāzī III, and has a less tapering return than that in ḥiǧāzī IV. It is close to the form of alif found in Sanaa DAM 01.30-1 , another possibly late ḥiǧāzī script showing a strong regularity of letter forms and line discipline, and also to that in St. Petersburg NLR, Marcel 18/2 ( Déroche 2014, figure 13 ) and Tübingen Ma VI 165 , whose script is categorised as B.Ia ( Déroche 2014, p. 132 ). The medial form of ǧīm/ḥā’/ḫāʾ has an oblique stroke that drops below the line, as is typical of ḥiǧāzī and B scripts. Final ǧīm/ḥā’/ḫā’ has an extended horizontal stroke that returns under the line in a short acute hook. This is a form seen in several examples of ḥiǧāzī script, such as that on Khalili Collection KFQ 59 , which is a fragment from the same Qur’an as BNF Arabe 334c and whose script is defined by François Déroche as ḥiǧāzī IV ( Déroche 1983, no. 9, pp. 36, 64, pl. VII ), and another transitional ḥiǧāzī/B type script on Staatsbibliothek, Berlin, ms.orient.Quart.1208(XI) . Extremely similar forms to the present examples are found repeatedly on coins minted between 690 and 710 ( Fraser 2012, pp. 131-2 ). The final form of ǧīm/ḥā’/ḫā’ also has similarities to certain examples found in BNF Arabe 331 , a ductus defined as B.Ia ( Déroche 1983, no. 15, p. 67, pl. IX ) and on Leiden University Library 14.545a , which is from the same manuscript as Paris BNF Arabe 335 , defined as B.Ib. Thus the form of the final ǧīm/ḥā’/ḫāʾ found on the present leaf may be a useful diagnostic of transitional scripts of the ḥiǧāzī/B groups. The form of the ‘ayn and ġayn vary somewhat but are most frequently similar to B.I and B.II. Final fā’/qāf is quite distinctive. The single example found on this folio (second line of the verso) has a tail below the line that has an emphatic arc to the left that then curves back to the right and bends to the left again in a tight hook at the base. Related forms can be found on manuscripts ranging from Sana‘a DAM 01-28.1 (ḥiǧāzī) Paris BNF Arabe 328d (B.Ia) and Istanbul TIEM 1186 (see Déroche 2014, pp.98, 103, fig. 29 , described as in Umayyad O I script). An even more curved version of this form is found on Khalili Collection KFQ 27 , a script designated as a version of B.I ( Déroche 1992, no. 7, p. 53 ). The fā’ has letter-pointing consisting of a single dot above the letter, whereas the qāf has no letter-pointing. The vertical element of final lām stretches far above and below the line, making it a very tall letter. A related characteristic is also found in ḥiǧāzī IV on BNF Arabe 334c and on Princeton University Library, Garret 14 G(a) , a ḥiǧāzī script that shows elements of B.I. Similar examples can also be found in some B scripts, such as that on QNL HC.MS.03190 and BNF Arabe 333b . However, the examples on the present leaf are stretched vertically more than all other examples. The lower horizontal stroke of the independent kāf is only just longer than the upper horizontal stroke (for a discussion of the kāf see Déroche 1999 ). In the lām-ʾalif combination, the ascenders converge slightly towards the top or sometimes rise in parallel, in a form that lies somewhere between ḥiǧāzī IV and B.Ia. Final mīm has a short tail that turns upwards slightly. It is essentially a B.Ia type, and sometimes the tail turns upwards slightly more emphatically, similar to examples in QNL HC.MS.03190 and BNF Arabe 333b . Final nūn varies slightly in its form. All examples have a tail that drops downwards below the line in quite a straight line or sloping very slightly to the left, terminating in a short hook return to the left. But in some examples the tail is rather long and has a more curved hook at the base, whereas others have a shorter more angular tail that resembles the equivalent letter form in B.II. Initial and medial form of hā’ is close to B.Ia. In most examples of the final yā’ the tail returns under the line in a straight horizontal stroke, but in some instances it has a very rounded form that curves forward in a large semi-circle. The two different forms can be seen clearly on lines 14-15 and 19-20 of the recto.

manuscripts.title: Vienna, Österreichische Nationalbibliothek, Cod. mixt. 917
manuscripts.repository: Österreichische Nationalbibliothek , Vienna
manuscripts.summary: Shelfmark: Cod. mixt. 917 ; 104 folios; Writing surface: Parchment; Qurʾānic text: Q 2:97-Q 7:205; Q 9:19-29; Script: kūfī (type B.II); Dimensions: 154 x 212 mm; Estimated date of production: circa 700-750 (?).
manuscripts.script: Josef von Karabacek suggests the 9th century as date for this large codex fragment (see below). In light of carbon dated manuscripts (Berlin, ms. or. fol. 4313, Tübingen Ma VI 165, and others) an earlier date estimate seems possible. The script shows features of early kūfī (type B.II) in the paleographic classification of François Déroche ( Oxford 1992 ). Josef von Karabacek, "Ein Koranfragment des IX. Jahrhunderts aus dem Besitze des Seldschukensultans Kaikubad", Sitzungsberichte der Kaiserlichen Akademie der Wissenschaften in Wien: Philosophisch-Historische Klasse, Bd. 6 "Zur orientalischen Altertumskunde", Wien 1917, pp. 11-13: "Der mit Rohrfeder in Sepiatinte ausgezogene Ductus ist ʿIrâkisch (al-ḫaṭṭ al-ʿirāqī), ein Lapidare in der Art des Kûfî (al-ḫaṭṭ al-kūfī), das zu einem Sammelnamen für jede alte steife, geradlinige Schrift geworden ist, welcher Name aber später selbst von arabischen Gelehrten ersten Ranges, wie z. B. al-Maḵrîzî (+ 1442), auf allerhand gesteifte und gekünstelte Lapidarzüge epigraphischer Denkmäler mißverständlich angewendet wurde, von unseren Orientalisten und Kunstgelehrten aber wahllos mißbraucht wird. Unter ʿIrâkischem Ductus ist ein allgemein provinzialer, vielleicht nicht ganz gleichmäßiger Schriftcharakter zu verstehen, der in den Maṣḥaf der verschiedenen Städte des ʿIrâk" [p. 12:] "gang und gäbe war, im Gegensatz zu dem al-kūfī und al-baṣrī, die im weiteren Sinne wohl auch ʿirâkisch sind, aber zwei ganz bestimmt Schreibschulen, die von al-Kûfa und al-Baṣra repräsentieren. Bisher sind alter sogenannte ,Kûfische' Koranmanuskripte auf Zeit und Herkunft palaeographisch noch nicht untersucht worden, wenigstens nicht wissenschaftlich methodisch. Die in den bestehenden Tafelpublikationen vorkommenden Bestimmungen können wohl kaum dafür gelten und datierte Maṣḥaf-Blätter sind ungemein selten. Ein bis zu einem gewissen Grade sicheres Hilfsmittel zur Lokalitätsbestimmung alter Korane gewähren unter anderem die Rezensionen der Texte, woraus sich gleichzeitig das entsprechende palaeographische Charakterbild ableiten läßt. Das ist z. B. mit den nordarabischen (Mekka- und Medîna-) Maṣḥafs der Fall, für außerdem noch der konstitutive Schriftcharakter des 7- und 8. Jahrhunderts quellenmäßig und urkundlich feststeht. Klar und zweifellos ergibt sich hie und da die lokale Bestimmung, wenigstens im weiteren Sinne, schon aus der prunkvollen Ornamentik der Sûrenteiler. Ein schönes Beispiel bietet ein Pergamentkorân der Vizeköniglichen Bibliothek zu Kairo, der in dem Tafelwerk ,Arabic Palaeography' Pl. 1-12, noch in das 1. Jahrhundert d. H. = 7. Jahrhundert n. Chr. Der Herausgeber ließ sich wohl das Alter gerade durch die Ornamentik vortäuschen: auf einer mit Ampeln behangenen Moschee-Kolonade und anderen Zierbändern siet man zwischen den Zinnen oder an den oberen Abschlußrändern verteilt, ornamentale Figuren, ähnlich der königlich geflügelten Krone mit dem Kugelbund aus der letzten Sasanidenzeit. Wohl diese Erscheinung mag es gewesen sein, die den Herausgeber zur Hinaufrückung der Datierung in das 7. Jahrhundert n. Chr. verleitete. Aber wie ist es denkbar, daß man einen Korân mit einer Zier hätte versehen können, wenn die Bedutung derselben als Herrscherattribut der feueranbetenden Könige bei den arabischen Eroberern noch lebendig ge- [p. 13] "wesen wäre?! Das Schriftbild lehrt unzweifelhaft, daß dieser Korân dem 3. Jahrhunder d. H., also dem 9. christlichen Jahrhundert angehören muß und das in die Zeit, wo altpersisches Wesen in den östlichen Provinzen des Chalifenreiches, wenn auch nur kurze Zeit, seine Renaissance feierte, so zwar, daß ihm, von dem Chalifen al-Mutawakkil ʿala Allâh (847-861 n.Chr.) ausgehende, auch äußerlich in Sitte und Tracht gehuldigt wurde. So ist diese auffällige ornamentale Erscheinung in dem Korân zu erklären, der folgerichtig einer persischen Schreibprovinz zuzweisen ist. Man wende nicht ein, daß die arabischen Eroberer das sasanidische Königsbild mitsamt den Emblemen des Feuerkults durch geraume Zeit auf ihren Geprägen in Persien und auf den Ispehbeden-Münzen in Taberistân sogar bis tief in das 2. Jahrhundert d. H. geduldet haben: das war dieselbe münzpolitische Tolerant, wie sie auch in ihrem westlichen Geldwesen gegenüber den Romäern geübt haben. Aber etwas anderes ist es, wenn das in Frage kommt, was den Muhammedanern am heiligsten und reinsten und nur für sie allein bestimmt ist, nämlich das durch ihren Propheten geoffenbarte Wort Gottes im Korân." The script of the manuscript is close to the early kūfī type B.II according to François Déroche's palaeographic classification from 1992 ( Oxford 1992 ). The letter ʾalif is always strictly vertical with a small hook at the baseline in the independent form. The letter group ǧīm/ḥāʾ/ḫāʾ in the medial form shows two different variants: in the first example the letter rests on the base line and does not cross the line vertically as in B.II manuscripts; in the second variant the bar of the letter goes across the base line. The final form has a short tail which ends in a short hook that is open to the right and is more similar to the B.I script style. The hook of the final form in B.II script style is longer and goes parallel to the base line. The letter ʿayn in the independent position has the same form of the tail as ǧīm. In the final position the letter is identical to the form shown in the B.I group with the angular tail. The final of isolated qāf recalls the shape of B.I. The letter hāʾ medially is thicker and rounder than those seen in B.I and B.II. The lām-ʾalif ligature is closer to B.II with the difference that the two antennas lean towards each other. The diacritical marking is made by oblique dots. The letter qāf is marked by a dot below the letter. The vocalisation is done by red dots. Typical letter shapes are presented in the palaeographic table, accessible as image no. 2 under fol. 1r. (Salome Beridze)

manuscripts.title: Philadelphia, Pennsylvania, Pennsylvania University Museum, E 16264 D
manuscripts.repository: Pennsylvania University Museum , Philadelphia, Pennsylvania
manuscripts.summary: 2 fol. on parchment; Shelfmark: E 16264 D; Qurʾānic Text: Q 16:2-16:14, Q 39:47-39:68; Script style: Fragment 1 : kūfī C.Ia, Fragment 2 : close to kūfī B.I; Estimated date of production: Fragment 1 700-800 CE; Fragment 2 bevor 750 CE.
manuscripts.script: Fragment 1 (fol. 1) is written in a style close to kūfī C.Ia influenced by the early kūfī script style B.I and ḥiǧāzī IV. The letter ʾalif resembles the C.Ia group. Within a word, the letters ǧīm/ḥāʾ/ḫāʾ cross the base line diagonally (not after a denticle) as those of B.I group, unlike those of C.Ia, where the letter rests on the base line. The letter ʿayn in the initial position is close to C.Ia but shows an interesting form of the upper hook: the hook has a rounded shape that looks like a dot, that cannot be seen in any other script groups. Within a word the letter resembles the B.Ib group, the right hand antenna being vertical. The letter qāf isolated or in the final position is similar to those of C.Ia group. The initial and medial forms of the letter hāʾ , alongside with the letter nūn in the final or isolated position and the letter mīm at the end of a word show a similar form seen in the ḥiǧāzī IV manuscript Saray 50385 from Istanbul, the script of which also shows similarities to the C.Ia group. As for the ligature lām-ʾalif , the form is similar to the C.Ia group. F. Déroche relates the script of the sister fragment BnF Arabe 332 to the C.Ia group ( See Déroche 1983: p. 77 ). The script of the second sister fragment, KFQ 49 from the Khalili Collections in London is labelled by Déroche as 'unclassified', although he links the manuscript with BnF Arabe 332 ( See Déroche 1992: p. 129 ). The manuscript shows occasional diacritical marking, but no signs of vocalisation. Fragment 2 (fol. 2): Due to the heavily damaged condition of fol. 2, limited amount of characters are available for the analysis of the script. The script of the second fragment is close to the early kūfī script style B.I. ʾAlif has a short lower return in the independent form, the shaft shows a slight inclination to the right. The form is close to B.Ib group. The letter ḥāʾ in the middle of a word on fol. 2v is written like a bar across the base line and is similar to the B.Ib group. In the independent position on fol. 2r the letter has a round generous hook which is different from the form attested in B.I. A similar form of the letters ǧīm/ḥāʾ/ḫāʾ in the indemendent position is seen in the ḥiǧāzī III manuscript Arabe 330 (a) . Mīm at the end of a word is similar to the B.Ia group with a tail that curves upwords. Nūn in the final or isolated position has a long vertical body with a short return at the bottom, which is similar to B.II. The ligature lām-ʾalif shows an interesting variant: In the independent porition it has a rounder base than in B.I forms. The two shafts of the letters curve towards each other as in B.Ia. In general, some of the features of the script such as ʾalif , mīm at the end of a word, nūn and the ligature lām-ʾalif are similar to the kūfī B fragments from Qatar National Library. Diacritics are indicated by short strokes. Vocalisation is made by red dots.

manuscripts.title: Doha, Qatar National Library (مكتبة قطرالوطنية), HC.MS.03190
manuscripts.repository: Qatar National Library (مكتبة قطرالوطنية) , Doha
manuscripts.summary: Single folio, Shelfmark: QNL HC.MS.03190; Qurʾānic Text: 18:60-86; Writing surface: Parchment; Lines per page: 16; Dimensions of leaf: 210 x 262 mm; Dimensions of text area: 16.5 x 22 cm ( Déroche 1983, p. 69, no. 21 ); 16.6 x 21.8 cm ( Déroche 1992, p. 52 ). Script style: kūfī, ductus of writing: B.Ib; Estimated date of production: circa 700-750. Verse markers: Single verse divisions are marked with a triangular cluster of three short dashes (type 1.1.4), each dash being the shape of the nib point of the calamus and written in the same brown ink as the text (for comparison of type see…
manuscripts.script: Diacritics: Letter-pointing: brown dots applied with the same ink as the text. Vocalisation: Red dots (possibly added later); There is evidence of re-inking on the flesh side (verso), but no obvious alterations to text. Style of script: B.Ib, an early, transitional kūfī. This Qur’an was probably written at a transitional period when kūfī script had been developed and was replacing ḥiǧāzī for the copying of the Qur’an. There is a much stronger regularity both in the letter-forms and the linear discipline of the base line than is seen in ḥiǧāzī scripts, however, there are letter forms that exhibit features related to ḥiǧāzī scripts and therefore may be diagnostic of an early date and transitional status. The final ǧīm/ḥā’/ḫā’ has a form associated with the early eighth century ( Fraser 2012 ; van Putten 2019 ). Although the present folio has only one occurrence of this letter form, which is a ḥāʾ , other folios from the same Qur’ān (listed above nos. 1-4) feature examples of ǧīm . On other folios from the same Qur’an (e.g. no. 2 above) the tail of the final ǧīm/ḥā’/ḫā’ is elongated back along the line. The final mīm often has an upturned terminus. The dot of the fā’ is placed above the letter, as is the usual case in later Arabic scripts, but the qāf in the present script is differentiated from the fā ’ by a single dot underneath the letter, rather than two dots above (as would be the case with later standard scripts). This aspect was noted by Déroche ( 1983, p. 69 ) and can be seen on the present folio. In many examples of ḥiǧāzī and kūfī scripts there was little or no original use of letter-pointing, but in some ḥiǧāzī scripts and those that may be called transitional early kūfī scripts, such as the present example, the differentiation of the fā’ and qāf varies. Sometimes the qāf is marked with a dot below, as here and in some examples in ḥiǧāzī and B I.a scripts (for examples see Farhad and Rettig 2016, pp. 142-3, 145 ; Déroche 1983, pl. IX ), but sometimes with a single dot above (e.g. Déroche 1992, p. 51 ). At other times the fā’ and the qāf are marked with single dots both above and below within a single manuscript (e.g. Cellard 2018, p. 10 ). In D type kūfī scripts (in examples where letter-pointing is present), the qāf is generally marked with two dots above (see Fraser and Kwiatkowski 2006, pp. 39-42 ), as is the case in later Arabic scripts. It is interesting to note that in Maghribi scripts of the 11th century onwards the qāf was marked by a dot above, whereas the fā’ was marked by a dot below, the reverse of the present example. The rules of how to demarcate these letters that share the same basic form were clearly somewhat fluid in the first centuries of Islam. The nib of the calamus used to write this script is relatively narrow and this is another feature of BI.a and BI.b scripts that is also found on a number of Qur’āns in ḥiǧāzī scripts (see, e.g., Déroche 2014, figs. 1-3, 5, 11, 12, 14, 16, 18 ; Déroche 1983, pl. IX ). The approximate shape of the nib-point can be deduced from the shape of the dots of the letter-pointing and those of the clusters marking the verse divisions. The scribe has not angled the nib to produce a varying width of stroke, but has kept the thickness of the strokes of the horizontal, vertical and diagonal parts of the letter forms uniform (see Gacek 2012, p. 41 for diagrams of nib-cuts; George 2010, p. 49 for illustration of technique). The script is regular, with consistent line discipline, but is not yet elegant or artistically evolved. It appears to be a transitional script between the ḥiǧāzī styles and the formal kūfī scripts of the later 8th and 9th centuries. Déroche ( 1992, pp. 35-36 ) notes that the letter forms of B.Ib are similar to those found in the earliest Islamic scripts, including inscriptions on stone dated 677-8 CE and of 8th century. François Déroche cautiously suggests a dating of 8th century for manuscripts employing B.Ib, but the somewhat archaic forms of the letters fā’/qāf, ǧīm/ḥā’/ḫā’ , dāl/ḏāl and lām-ʾalif suggest a date in the first half of the 8th century ( Fraser 2006 , Fraser 2012 ). As Déroche further notes ( 1992, p. 35 ), the codicological aspects of Qur’āns in B.Ib are varied and the page-shape formats include vertical, square and horizontal, again indicating a stage of transition between the vertical format of the earliest Qur’ans in ḥiǧāzī scripts and the more consistent horizontal format of Qur’ans in mature kūfī script. This aspect also supports a date of production in the first half of the 8th century, equivalent to the later Umayyad dynasty. George suggests that B.I scripts were early kūfī developments that had evolved by the early 8th century and were transitory between ḥiǧāzī and mature kūfī and, referring to a folio in the Khalili Collection (no. 3 above), suggests that the specific script represented here was an early example of the B.I style and that the manuscript from which the present folio originates could be attributed to the early 8th century ( 2010, p. 42, fig. 22, p. 91 ). The present script is a close relative of a script on a Qur’an in the Museum of Turkish and Islamic Arts, Istanbul (previously held in the Umayyad Mosque of Damascus) described by some scholars as a ḥiǧāzī script (see Farhad and Rettig 2016, no. 2, pp. 144-5) and by others as B.Ib and dating possibly from the end of the Umayyad period ( Déroche 2014, p. 132, fig. 45 ). Not only is the general style of the two scripts very similar, but certain individual letter forms are almost identical, such as the final ǧīm/ḥā’/ḫā’ , dāl, mīm, hā’, and, importantly, it also shares the same sub-linear placement of the dot of the qāf . Furthermore, the width of the nib and the square format are also similar in both examples.

manuscripts.title: Doha, Qatar National Library (مكتبة قطرالوطنية), HC.MS.03193
manuscripts.repository: Qatar National Library (مكتبة قطرالوطنية) , Doha
manuscripts.summary: Script: kūfī, ductus: Close to B.Ia; Dimensions: Current dimensions of fragmentary leaf: 207 x 283 mm; Estimated date of production: circa 700-750. Notes on the dimensions: Estimated dimensions of original complete leaf: 340-360 mm x 290-310 mm; Estimated dimensions of original complete text area: 270 x 240 mm The folio is damaged at the top and bottom edges. There are 19 surviving lines on each side, fragmentary at upper and lower edges, with visible elements of at least one more line at the upper and lower edges. As a result 15 verses of Qur’ānic text have been lost from this folio. Based…
manuscripts.script: Diacritics: most of the letters that require diacritics in standard scripts have the requisite points here, except for the yā’ , which is never pointed. The individual dots are applied in the same brown ink as the script and are formed by placing the nib of the stylus once on the parchment at a diagonal angle (i.e. we can see the approximate shape of the nib of the stylus from the shape of the letter-points). On letters that require more than one point (e.g. tā’, ṯā’ etc), the dots are placed one above the other at a slight diagonal, but this line of dots can vary from near vertical to near horizontal. For example, the letter ṯā’ in line 9 of the verso has an almost vertical line of dots above the letter, whereas in line 17 of the verso it extends at a shallow angle approaching the horizontal. For the letter šīn, a single dot is placed above each denticle so that the three points are effectively arranged in a horizontal row above the letter. For the letters fā’ and qāf , in most instances of the letter fā’ there is a dot placed above the circular letter body (as in standard scripts), but there are a few instances where the dots are absent. In contrast, the letter qāf is never pointed on this folio. The vowels signs are indicated with red dots and green dots. The script of this Qur’ān is perhaps a transitional script between late ḥiǧāzī scripts and kūfī scripts. There is considerable line discipline on the verso, but on the recto this is less consistent and the lines sag slightly in the central area and then slant upwards towards the end of the line. Most of the letter forms are closest to style B.Ia, but there are some individual letter forms that are closer to A.I, B.Ib and B.II. For example, the final nūn is generally closest to B.Ib, but certain examples are closer to A.I or B.II, whereas none are close to B.Ia. There is also variation in the form of different instances of the same letter. For example, the ʾalifs generally accord with script type B.Ia, but the exact form varies considerably, with a number of examples having a shorter ascender and others having a more rounded curve, while some slope slightly forward rather than backwards, the latter which Déroche states is a diagnostic feature of B.Ia ( Déroche 1992, p. 38 ). The lām-ʾalif combination is closest to B.Ia but there is some inconsistency in the exact form, with some instances having ascenders that diverge slightly. Some examples of ṭāʾ and ẓāʾ are close to B.Ia while others are closer to B.II; the letters ‘ayn and ġayn are closest to B.Ia; the final qāf is closest to B.Ia, but the exact forms varies slightly; the final mīm is closest to B.Ia. Many of the ascenders slant to the right, a characteristic of ḥiǧāzī scripts (see Déroche 1992, p. 35 ). The ascenders of ʾalif and lām when used in conjunction, as in the word Allah, are not parallel but slant inwards towards each other, i.e the ʾalif slopes slightly to the left and the lam slopes slightly to the right. This is an interesting variation on the B script types and may be a further indication that the present script was produced at a formative stage of B script evolution. These multiple inconsistencies and variations in letter forms on a single folio, along with the (original) vertical format and form of the verse markers, may be diagnostic of a transitional status between ḥiǧāzī the codified regularity of kūfī scripts, and therefore points to a date in the first half of the eighth century (for a discussion of B scripts and their relation to each other and to ḥiǧāzī scripts, see Déroche 1992, pp. 35-6, 38-9 ). It is also interesting to note that a correction to the text has been added using a form of the letter ʾalif that is archaic: on line 14 of the verso the spelling of the word balā’un has an ʾalif added at the end in a different hand and a different ink from the main script, and this added ʾalif slants to the right and has a very short lower tail, a form that is close to some ḥiǧāzī scripts such as those found on the Codex Amrensis (see e.g. Déroche 1992, no. 2, p. 31 ; Déroche 2014, fig. 18 ). This suggests that the correction was inserted at a time when some ḥiǧāzī scripts were still in use in certain contexts, since the scribe who made the correction did not attempt to replicate the form of ʾalif used in the main script of this folio nor use a letter-form associated with slightly later script types such as D. This suggests that the original B.Ia script of this folio must also have been coeval with an ongoing or belated use of ḥiǧāzī scripts. This would also accord with the somewhat archaic form of the red script used for the sura heading.

manuscripts.summary: Folio from an Umayyad Qurʾān codex; Shelfmark: HC.MS.03202; Writing surface: parchment; Writing material: Ink and opaque watercolour on parchment; Qurānic text: 43:49-73, Lines per page: 20; Script style: kūfī, type C; Dimensions: 490 x 425 mm; Estimated date: 700-750. Written on parchment in brown ink, the text is placed into a decorated frame, a new feature of text design compared to other ancient codices. manuscripts.script: /* Style Definitions */ table.MsoNormalTable {mso-style-name:"Normale Tabelle"; mso-tstyle-rowband-size:0; mso-tstyle-colband-size:0; mso-style-noshow:yes; mso-style-priority:99; mso-style-parent:""; mso-padding-alt:0cm 5.4pt 0cm 5.4pt; mso-para-margin-top:0cm; mso-para-margin-right:0cm; mso-para-margin-bottom:10.0pt; mso-para-margin-left:0cm; line-height:115%; mso-pagination:widow-orphan; font-size:11.0pt; font-family:"Calibri","sans-serif"; mso-ascii-font-family:Calibri; mso-ascii-theme-font:minor-latin; mso-hansi-font-family:Calibri; mso-hansi-theme-font:minor-latin; mso-fareast-language:EN-US;} Der Schriftstil der zwölf Blätter (Fragment B = fol. 2-fol. 13) ist nach Déroche als kūfī (Typ B.I.b) zu klassifizieren (Déroche 1999, S. 66f.) und zeigt bei der Form des nach rechts geneigten Buchstaben ʾalif ein Kennzeichen der ḥiǧāzī -Schrift. Das erste Blatt (Fragment A = fol. 1) der im Mausoleum aufbewahrten Handschrift, besteht aus zwei übereinandergeklebten kufischen Bätter im Querformat, die in einem Schriftstil des Typs kūfī D IV verfasst ist.

manuscripts.script: Die Grundschrift, die zu einem späteren Zeitpunkt mit einer dunkleren Tinte nachgezeichnet wurde, notiert Buchstaben, diakritische Punkte und Verstrenner. In roter Tinte sind Vokalzeichen in Form von Punkten und die Überschrift der 9. Sure eingetragen: fātiḥat sūrat at-tauba wa-hiya miʾa wa-ṯalaṯūna ʾāya , "Sure at-Tauba und sie [hat] 130 Verse". Die Surenüberschrift ist in einem ähnlichem Schriftstil wie der Grundtext notiert.

manuscripts.title: Meknes, Private library of Sherif ʿAbdarraḥmān b. Zīdān, no call number
manuscripts.repository: Private library of Sherif ʿAbdarraḥmān b. Zīdān , Meknes
manuscripts.summary: 380 parchment folios; no shelfmark known; Qurʾānic Text: Q 2 - Q 108 (the text of fol. 2r, presumably Q 2:1-6, is hardly recognizable); Lines per page: 17; Script style: kūfī (type C.I); Dimensions: 210 x 285 mm; Estimated date of production: 700-800.
manuscripts.script: Die kräftig aufgetragene braune Schrift ist teilweise mit roten Punkten vokalisiert; die Farbe läßt sich zwar aufgrund der Schwarz-Weiß-Aufnahmen nicht genau bestimmen, anhand einiger Farbphotos von Fragmenten in Kuweit (Tāriq-Raǧab-Museum) und des Auktionshauses Bonhams (Auktion 2008), lässt sich die Farbe der Tinte, Punkte und Verstrenner jedoch erkennen ( Webseite von Brannon Wheeler; Christies, Sale 7959, Art of the Islamic & Indian Worlds, 7 April 2011, London, King Street, Lot 1 and Lot 2; Christies, Sale 5526, Arts & Textiles of the Islamic & Indian Worlds, 10 October 2014, London, South Kensington, Lot 250; Christies, Sale 10490, Art of the Islamic and Indian Worlds, 23 April 2015, London King Street, Lot 12; Christies, Sale 10386 Art of the Islamic and Indian Worlds, 8 October 2015, London King Street, Lot 2 ). Nur vereinzelt werden Buchstaben gelängt ( mašq ), im allgemeinen haben sie eine einheitliche Größe und halten regelmäßigen Abstand zueinander. Die Schrift enthält vereinzelt diakritische Zeichen, sehr häufig beim Buchstaben nūn . Bei einer Stichprobe von drei Seiten (fol. 68v, fol. 145v und fol. 299r) wurde festgestellt,dass von 151mehrdeutigen Konsonanten 16 mit diakritischen Punkten versehen sind (fol. 68v), 136 Konsonanten (fol. 145v) und 124 Buchstaben, die ein diakritisches Zeichen besitzen sollten, 16, 20 bzw. 10 davon in der älteren Schicht diakritische Zeichen aufweisen (ca. 11% der mehrdeutigen Buchstaben). Vokale sind durch rote Punkte notiert, wobei sich nachweisen lässt, dass ein roter Punkt bei den Buchstaben ʾalif , wāw oder yā auch den Verschlusslaut (arab. hamza ), s. z.B. Q 22:55, Q 19:39; Q 19:27 oder Q 19:28. Ein Punkt auf der rechten Seite des ʾalif scheint ein kurzes /a/ zu notieren, ein Punkt auf der linken Seite des Buchstaben hingegeben den Langvokal /ā/ . An einigen Stellen wird durch die roten Punkte die Schreibung der ʾ imāla ("Vokalflexion", = Aussprache eines a-Vokals "in Richtung / e/ oder / i/ ") notiert. Diese wird mit einem Punkt unterhalb des Buchstaben, vor dem ʾ alif gekennzeichnet (z.B. Q 18:29). Die Punktierung des Kodex folgt einem einheitlichen System. Die späteren Bearbeitungen betreffen zunächst die Verstrennungszeichen. Die ursprünglichen Verstrenner, vier übereinander bemalte Striche (z.B. 020:003), wurden später an vielen Stellen mit einem pyramidenförmigen Zeichen aus Gold übermalt. Außerdem manche diakritischen Zeichen und Vokalzeichen von einer späteren Hand ergänzt bzw. korrigiert (z.B. Q 18:51, Q 18:16). Da die später eingesetzten Punkte größer und dunkler sind, lassen sie sich von den Zeichen der ersten Textschicht unterscheiden. Textergänzungen an den durch Schäden fehlenden Ecken und die Kustoden wurden später vorgenommen, wie es an der späteren Schriftform erkennbar ist (z.B. fol. 19v/20r). Der Schrifttyp der Handschrift lässt sich als kūfī (Typ C.I) der von François Déroche entwickelten Paläographie klassifizieren.

manuscripts.title: Paris, Bibliothèque nationale de France, Arabe 332
manuscripts.repository: Bibliothèque nationale de France , Paris
manuscripts.summary: Qurʾān fragment on parchment; Shelfmark Arabe 332; 49 fol; 21 lines per page; Script style kūfī type C.Ia influenced by ḥiǧāzī IV and the early kūfī script style B.I.
manuscripts.script: This manuscript represents a script style close to kūfī type C.Ia influenced by the early kūfī script style B.I and ḥiǧāzī IV . The letter ʾalif resembles the C.Ia group. Within a word, the letters ǧīm/ḥāʾ/ḫāʾ cross the (_test _test 2019: PN) base line diagonally (not after a denticle) as those of B.I group, unlike those of C.Ia, where the letter rests on the base line. The tail of the letter in the final position is horizontal as those of C.Ia. The letter ʿayn in the initial position is close to C.Ia but shows an interesting form of the upper hook: the hook has a rounded shape that looks like a dot, that cannot be seen in any other script groups. Within a word the letter resembles the B.Ib group, the right hand antenna being vertical. The final form of the letter is identical to those seen in the D.IV group, but is also close to the B.I/B.II group. The letter qāf isolated or in the final position is similar to those of C.Ia group. The initial and medial forms of the letter hāʾ , alongside with the letter nūn in the final or isolated position and the letter mīm at the end of a word show a similar form seen in the ḥiǧāzī IV manuscript Saray 50385 from Istanbul, the script of which also shows similarities to the C.Ia group. As for the ligature lām-ʾalif , the form is similar to the C.Ia group. F. Déroche relates the script of the fragment BnF Arabe 332 to the C.Ia group ( See Déroche 1983: p. 77 ). The script of the second sister fragment, KFQ 49 from the Khalili Collections in London is categorized by Déroche as 'unclassified', although he links the manuscript with BnF Arabe 332 ( See Déroche 1992: p. 129 ). The manuscript shows occasional diacritical marking, but no signs of vocalisation. Typical letter shapes are presented in the palaeographic table, accessible as image no. 2 under fol. 1r.

manuscripts.title: Istanbul, Topkapı Palace Museum (Topkapı Sarayı Müzesi), "Saray 50385" (Gotthelf Bergsträßer archives)
manuscripts.repository: Topkapı Palace Museum (Topkapı Sarayı Müzesi) , Istanbul
manuscripts.summary: 353 fol. (according to Gotthelf Bergsträßer archives); Parchment; Shelfmark: "Saray 50385" (according to Gotthelf Bergsträßer archives); Qurʾānic text: Q 1-14:9, Q 22:44 - Q 114; 15-19 lines per page; Dimensions: 360 x 290 mm; Script style corresponds to ḥiǧāzī IV (typology of F. Déroche, 1983 ) see palaeography by Salome Beridze; Estimated date of production: 700-800 (?).
manuscripts.script: The script of this manuscript corresponds with ḥiǧāzī IV style and shows some similarities with kūfī style B.I . The vertical characters of the script slant to the right. The ʾalif is similar to ḥiǧāzī IV and also to B.Ia. The initial form of the letter group ǧīm/ḥāʾ/ḫāʾ is close to ḥiǧāzī IV, as is the medial form of the letter. In the final position the letter shows a long horizontal tail with a short final loop as in ḥiǧāzī IV. However, the final loop is abscent in some examples. The initial ʿain shows a variation from ḥiǧāzī IV , the upper hook is rounder, that resembles the kūfī C group. When the letter occurs at the end of a word, the form is closer to the B.I style. When isolated, it shows a similar form to the final ǧīm/ḥāʾ/ḫāʾ group with a horizontal tail with a short final loop. The final qāf has a long vertical tail and is close to the B.I style. The letter mīm is identical with ḥiǧāzī IV. The final or isolated nūn resembles the ḥiǧāzī IV style, as is the letter hāʾ . The lām-ʾalif ligature is close to B.Ia and ḥiǧāzī IV with the two vertical lines curving to each other. Although the script style of the manuscript shows some influence of kūfī C style and B.I, it seems to be a manuscript of ḥiǧāzī IV style according to the typology of François Déroche, 1983 . Diacritical marks are used occasionally. The letters nūn , bāʾ and fāʾ are marked quite often. The qāf is marked with a dot below the letter. The vocalisation is made by presumably red dots, as we only have access to the black and white photos from the Bergsträßer photo archive. (Salome Beridze)

manuscripts.script: Die Tintenfarbe ist auf den vorliegenden Schwarz-Weiß-Photographien nur schlecht erkennbar, wahrscheinlich ist sie schwarz oder dunkelbraun. Die Schrift weist durchgehend diakritische Zeichen auf, Vokalzeichen sind in einer helleren Tinte, wahrscheinlich in rot eingetragen. Es handelt sich um eine Handschrift die aus verschiedenen Teilen zusammengesetzt wurde, mindestens vier Schriftstile lassen sich nachweisen.

manuscripts.script: Das Schriftbild der ältesten Schicht, die an einigen Stellen (z.B. fol. 548v) noch sehr gut zu erkennen ist, lässt sich dem Schriftstil der B-Gruppe nach der Typologie von François Déroche (vgl. Déroche 1983 u. Déroche 1992) zuordnen. Der Schriftstil dieser Gruppe zeichnet sich u.a. durch vertikale Hasten und die Schreibung des Buchstaben hāʾ in mittlerer Position aus, das unter die Grundlinie greift. Im Duktus der Handschrift knickt der Buchstabe nūn in Endposition nach links ab, wie es für die Schriftstile des Typs B I b und B II ( ʿirāqī ) typisch ist. Die in schwarzer Tinte gehaltene Linie der Handschrift ist von mittlerer Dicke, wobei manche Blätter (z.B. fol. 1103 oder fol. 1104) in einem Duktus mit auffallender Schreibung des nūn in Endposition und der Buchstaben fāʾ bzw. qāf in mittlerer Position nachbearbeitet wurden. Die ursprüngliche Schrift notiert diakritische Zeichen. Die Handschrift scheint ebenfalls in dunkler Tinte Vokalpunkte zu enthalten.

Leider bleibt unbekannt, wieviele Blätter die Handschrift insgesamt enthält, welche Signatur sie hat und wo genau sie sich damals befindet. Auch die…

manuscripts.title: Cairo, Khedivial Library, today: Egyptian National Library and Archives (دار الكتب والوثائق القومية), Moritz 1905, table 43
manuscripts.repository: Khedivial Library, today: Egyptian National Library and Archives (دار الكتب والوثائق القومية) , Cairo
manuscripts.summary: Die beiden im Paläographie-Album von Bernhard Moritz (1905) abgebildeten, stark beschädigten Papyrusfragmente, gehörten ursprünglich zusammen, wie an dem Wort allaḏīna ( الدٮں ) erkennbar ist, das in beiden Fragmenten ähnlich geschrieben ist. Das Papyrusblatt enthielt vermutlich einen Abschnitt aus Sure 28, vielleicht von Vers 48 bis Vers 85 (?), wobei der Textabschnitt des linken Fragments anhand der lesbaren Wörter (Zeile 1: هم الحٯ ;Zeile 2: اوتى ; Zeile 6: الدٮں ا ; Zeile 7: مٮا ٮه ; Zeile 8: ٮوتون اجر ; Zeile 9: ينفقوں Zeile 10: اعملٮا ) gut zum Textbereich Vers 48-57 der 28. Sure passen…
manuscripts.script: Die sehr beschädigten Fragmentstücke enthalten nur wenig lesbaren Text. Die wenigen, in recht dicker Tinte geschrieben Wörter haben diakritische Zeichen, anscheinend wird das fāʾ durch einen Punkt unter dem Buchstaben und das qāf durch einen Punkt über dem Buchstaben notiert (vgl. linke Abbildung, Zeile 9: ينفقوں yunfiqūna). In der gleichen Zeile ist ein aus drei Strichen gesetzter Verstrenner erkennbar, wie er in Koranhandschriften üblicherweise gesetzt wird. Vokalpunkte scheinen nicht vorzukommen. Die Endform der Buchstaben nūn, qāf und yāʾ sind Kennzeichen einer frühen Schriftform.

manuscripts.title: Paris, Bibliothèque nationale de France, Arabe 325 (a)
manuscripts.repository: Bibliothèque nationale de France , Paris
manuscripts.summary: 12 fol. on parchment; Shelfmark: Arabe 325 (a); Qurʾānic text: Q 13:7-16:73, Q 26:132-199, Q 27:50-28:19, Q 28:71-29:7; 18 lines per page; This fragment and the manuscript Ms. orient. A 443 from Forschungsbibliothek Gotha originally belonged to the same Qurʾān codex. The script is very close to the script of the manuscripts: Paris Arabe 333 (b) from the Bibliothèque national de France and Doḥa HC.MS.03190 from the Qatar National Library collection, alongside with other four sister fragments: M.2002.1.385 , Christie's, lot 24 , KFQ 20 and E 16264 N . Fraser considers Arabe 325 (a) and Ms.…
manuscripts.script: The text is written in the early kūfī script style B.Ib, representing the transitional period between the kūfī and ḥiǧāzī . The ʾalif is strictly vertical, as well as other vertical characters of the script. The initial ʿayn has more resemblance with the style B.Ia as it has rather a rounded form than angular as in B.Ib. Diacritical marks are used frequently, indicated by short oblique dashes. There is another layer of diacritics made by a later hand, marking mostly the letter yāʾ with two dots. The letter fāʾ is marked with a dot above, the qāf is originally left unmarked, a later hand marks the letter with two dots above. Vocalisation is made by red dots, hamza is indicated by a green dot. Often small lines are placed at the end of a line to fill out the space.

manuscripts.title: Chicago, Institute for the Study of Ancient Cultures, A 6992
manuscripts.repository: Institute for the Study of Ancient Cultures , Chicago
manuscripts.summary: 1 fol. on parchment; Shelfmark: A 6992; Qurʾānic Text: Q 6:139-145; 12 lines per page (originally unknown); Script style ḥiǧāzī IV; Estimated date of production: 700-800 AD.
manuscripts.script: The script of this Qurʾān manuscript is close to the ḥiǧāzī IV style (according to the typologiy of François Déroche ). The shaft of ʾalif and other vertical upstrokes slant to the right, that is a characteristic of ḥiǧāzī scripts. The letter ǧīm initially is similar to the ḥiǧāzī IV group. When in the middle of a word, the letter is shaped like a short oblique bar crossing the base line. This form is found in ḥiǧāzī manuscripts as well as in early kūfī B.I fragments. ʿAyn in the initial form is similar to ḥiǧāzī IV with its longer base line and small hook. When isolated the letter shows an interesting feature: the horizontal tail rests on the base line and does not end in a small hook as in ḥiǧāzī IV style. This kind of shape is attested in ḥiǧāzī I and kūfī A.I scripts. Mīm in the middle of a word is circular and goes slightly below the base line. In the final or independent form the letter has a short horizontal tail that curves upwards at the and similar to ḥiǧāzī III/IV and kūfī B.Ia script styles. The body of the nūn is not as vertical as in ḥiǧāzī IV style and is more similar to ḥiǧāzī III and kūfī B.Ia script styles. Hāʾ in the medial position is similar to ḥiǧāzī IV, the bar is vertical, the 'eyes' are defined, but the letter goes slightly below the base line. Lām-ʾalif ligature is similar to ḥiǧāzī IV, where the lām is thinner than the ʾalif . Overall, the script of the fragment A 6992 might represent a transitional stage of the development of the Arabic script between ḥiǧāzī and early kūfī . Diacritical marks are represented in the shape of oblique dashes. Traces of red dots for the vocalisation are attested but scarcely. Some usage of black ink is seen on both sides of the folio to mark the modern vocalisation system and for adding diacritical marks supposedly at a later stage. (Salome Beridze)

manuscripts.title: Chicago, Institute for the Study of Ancient Cultures, A 7001

manuscripts.title: Chicago, Institute for the Study of Ancient Cultures, A 6958
manuscripts.repository: Institute for the Study of Ancient Cultures , Chicago
manuscripts.summary: Single folio on parchment; Shelfmark: A 6958; Qurʾānic text: Q 5:91-5:94, Q 5:97-5:102; 11 lines per page (originally probably 20 lines); Script style: kūfī B.Ib.
manuscripts.script: This manuscript represents the early kūfī script style B.Ib. The main difference between the styles B.Ia and B.Ib is that in case of the B.Ia group the shaft of ʾalif and other vertical strokes slope to the right ( See Déroche, 1992 ). The letter ʾalif in the manuscript is vertical. As for the letter group ǧīm/ḥāʾ/ḫāʾ in the isolated position, they show resemblance to the script style ḥiǧāzī IV as the horizontal tail is alongated and ends in a short hook. The final letter ʿayn also shares features with the ḥiǧāzī IV group, the body of the letter is more pronounced and angular, than in B.I. In the initial position, the letter shows a round upper curve closer to the B.II group. The initial ʿayn of the B.I group has a short angular hook. The same is the case for the letter in the medial position: the two antennas are hardly distinguishable, the right hand antenna being vertical, as in B.II. The qāf isolated or in the final position shows again a different form, the body is more cursive and less vertical than in B.I. Oblique strokes are marking the diacritica of the script. The qāf is marked by a dot below the letter, as in most early kūfī Qur'an manuscripts. The vocalisation is done by red dots. (Salome Beridze)

manuscripts.title: London, The Khalili Collections, KFQ 49
manuscripts.repository: The Khalili Collections , London
manuscripts.summary: Single folio Qurʾān fragment on parchment; Shelfmark KFQ 49; 21 lines per page; Script style kūfī type C.Ia influenced by ḥiǧāzī IV and the early kūfī script style B.I;
manuscripts.script: This manuscript represents a script style close to kūfī type C.Ia influenced by the early kūfī script style B.I and ḥiǧāzī IV . The letter ʾalif resembles the C.Ia group. Within a word, the letters ǧīm/ḥāʾ/ḫāʾ cross the base line diagonally (not after a denticle) as those of B.I group, unlike those of C.Ia, where the letter rests on the base line. The tail of the letter in the final position is horizontal as those of C.Ia. The letter ʿayn in the initial position is close to C.Ia but shows an interesting form of the upper hook: the hook has a rounded shape that looks like a dot, that cannot be seen in any other script groups. Within a word the letter resembles the B.Ib group, the right hand antenna being vertical. The final form of the letter is identical to those seen in the D.IV group, but is also close to the B.I/B.II group. The letter qāf isolated or in the final position is similar to those of C.Ia group. The initial and medial forms of the letter hāʾ , alongside with the letter nūn in the final or isolated position and the letter mīm at the end of a word show a similar form seen in the ḥiǧāzī IV manuscript Saray 50385 from Istanbul, the script of which also shows similarities to the C.Ia group. As for the ligature lām-ʾalif , the form is similar to the C.Ia group. F. Déroche relates the script of the sister fragment BnF Arabe 332 to the C.Ia group ( See Déroche 1983: p. 77 ). The script of the fragment KFQ 49 is categorized by Déroche as 'unclassified', although he links the manuscript with BnF Arabe 332 ( See Déroche 1992: p. 129 ). The manuscript shows occasional diacritical marking, but no signs of vocalisation.
