manuscripts.title: Doha, Museum of Islamic Art (متحف الفن الإسلامي), MS.276
manuscripts.repository: Museum of Islamic Art (متحف الفن الإسلامي) , Doha
manuscripts.summary: Single leaf Qur'an manuscript on parchment; Shelfmark: MS.276; 20 lines per page; script style: kūfī B.Ib.
manuscripts.script: This manuscript represents the early kūfī script style B.Ib. The main difference between the styles B.Ia and B.Ib is that in case of the B.Ia group the shaft of ʾalif and other vertical strokes slope to the right ( See Déroche, 1992 ). The letter ʾalif in the manuscript is vertical. As for the letter group ǧīm/ḥāʾ/ḫāʾ in the isolated position, they show resemblance to the script style ḥiǧāzī IV as the horizontal tail is alongated and ends in a short hook. The final letter ʿayn also shares features with the ḥiǧāzī IV group, the body of the letter is more pronounced and angular, than in B.I. In the initial position, the letter shows a round upper curve closer to the B.II group. The initial ʿayn of the B.I group has a short angular hook. The same is the case for the letter in the medial position: the two antennas are hardly distinguishable, the right hand antenna being vertical, as in B.II. The qāf isolated or in the final position shows again a different form, the body is more cursive and less vertical than in B.I. Oblique strokes are marking the diacritica of the script. The qāf is marked by a dot below the letter, as in most early kūfī Qur'an manuscripts. The vocalisation is done by red dots.

manuscripts.title: Rome, Biblioteca Apostolica Vaticana, Vat. ar. 1784
manuscripts.repository: Biblioteca Apostolica Vaticana , Rome
manuscripts.summary: 10 fol. on parchment; Shelfmark Vat. ar. 1784; Qurʾānic Text: Q 73:18-75:26, Q 75:28-87:3, Q 87:7-96:3; 21 lines per page; Dimensions: 350 x 428mm; Script style kūfī type C.Ia influenced by ḥiǧāzī IV and the early kūfī script style B.I; Estimated date of production: 700-800 CE.
manuscripts.script: This manuscript represents a script style close to kūfī type C.Ia influenced by the early kūfī script style B.Iand ḥiǧāzī IV . The letter ʾalif resembles the C.Ia group. Within a word, the letters ǧīm/ḥāʾ/ḫāʾ cross the base line diagonally as those of B.I group, unlike those of C.Ia, where the letter rests on the base line. The tail of the letter in the final position is horizontal as those of C.Ia. The letter ʿayn in the initial position is close to C.Ia but shows an interesting form of the upper hook: the hook has a rounded shape that looks like a dot, that cannot be seen in any other script groups. Within a word the letter resembles the B.Ib group, the right hand antenna being vertical. The final form of the letter is identical to those seen in the D.IV group, but is also close to the B.I/B.II group. The letter qāf isolated or in the final position is similar to those of C.Ia group. The initial and medial forms of the letter hāʾ , alongside with the letter nūn in the final or isolated position and the letter mīm at the end of a word show a similar form seen in the ḥiǧāzī IV manuscript Saray 50385 from Istanbul, the script of which also shows similarities to the C.Ia group. As for the ligature lām-ʾalif , the form is similar to the C.Ia group. F. Déroche relates the script of the sister fragment BnF Arabe 332 to the C.Ia group ( See Déroche 1983: p. 77 ). The script of the second sister fragment, KFQ 49 from the Khalili Collections in London is categorized by Déroche as 'unclassified', although he links the manuscript with Arabe 332 ( See Déroche 1992: p. 129 ). The manuscript shows occasional diacritical marking, but no signs of vocalisation.

manuscripts.summary: Single folio parchment Qurʾān manuscript with the shelfmark M.2002.1.351. Qurʾān text: Q 54:31-55:8. At the moment only the image of the fol 1 recto is given by the institution.
manuscripts.script: Script style kūfi B.II with some variations. The letter ʾalif slightly bands to the right. The tail of the letter ʿayn in the final position extends generously. The lām-ʾalif ligature has more resemblance with the shape seen in the B.Ib style. Diacritical marks are used sparsely and are indicated by oblique strokes. Vocalisation is marked by red and blue (?) dots.

manuscripts.title: Tübingen, Universitätsbibliothek Tübingen, Ma VI 166
manuscripts.repository: Universitätsbibliothek Tübingen , Tübingen
manuscripts.summary: 30 fol. on parchment; Shelfmark: Ma VI 166; Qur'anic text: Q 14:17 - 20:117; 15 lines per page; Dimensions: 109 x 156mm; Horizontal format; Script style: Early kūfī B.Ib.
manuscripts.script: The script of this Qur'an manuscript represents the early kūfī script style B.Ib with some variations. The letter ʾalif is always vertical. In the independent form it has a short lower return that looks like a hook, as in B.II style. The letter ǧīm in the initial form is close to the B.I group. Medially this letter is similar to the B.Ib group with an oblique bar that goes across the base line. The tail of the final ǧīm goes upwards before ending in a small hook that is open to the right. The independent form of the letter ʿayn shows the same form of the tail and is similar to the B.I group in all positions.The letter qāf in isolated or final position is also similar to the B.I group, the tail is sometimes more elongated. The body of the letter nūn is more angular than in the B.Ib group. The lām-ʾalif ligature resembles the from in the B.II group, only that the shafts are longer and thinner than in the B.II style. The manuscript uses the maš q method, which is the elongation of the horizontal components of the script. Diacritical marking is used very scarcely in the manuscript. The vocalisation is made by red dots.

manuscripts.summary: Der Katalog der Pariser Koranhandschriften (Déroche 1983: p. 78) enthält folgende Angaben: "Arabe 337 b : ffo 7 à 9. Du même manuscrit que le fragment précédent. Fo 7 : II, 282-111, 5 ; fo 8 à 9 : III, 30-58. Copie anonyme et non datée. Graphie C I a. Diacritiques rares traits obliques ; vocalisation : points rouges, partiellement portés. Des groupes de traits obliques (1.1.1) séparent les versets; des alif mi-violet, mi-bleu indiquent les groupes de cinq versets, des cercles mi-violet mi-rouge de type 1.A.I ceux de dix. Parchemin. 2 feuillets. Page : 144 x 204. 16 lignes. Encre brun noir.…

manuscripts.title: Doha, Qatar National Library (مكتبة قطرالوطنية), HC.MS.03156
manuscripts.repository: Qatar National Library (مكتبة قطرالوطنية) , Doha
manuscripts.summary: 4 folios,Shelfmark: HC.MS.03156 , HC.MS.03157 , HC.MS.03158 (2 fol.); Text: HC.MS.03156 029:032:013-029:050:004; HC.MS.03157 : 030:024:018-030:043:007; HC.MS.03158 (fol. 1) 028:029:013-028:046:019; HC.MS.03158 (fol. 2) 028:046:020-028:064:013. Lines per page: 17 Dimensions: HC.MS.03158 : text area 135 x 182 mm; with mount 169 x 238 mm; HC.MS.03156 : 135 x 182 mm; with mount: 169 x 238 mm; HC.MS.03157 : 135 x 182 mm; with mount: 169 x 238 mm; Script stye: kūfī, Ductus: B.II, with some variations. Estimated date of production: c. 750-800. Single verse separators: a short column of angled ink…
manuscripts.script: Diacritics: Letter-pointing consists of angled dashes in the same ink as the main script. Diacritics (vocalisation) are marked with red and green dots. Discussion of the script: Most letter forms accord with B.II. Those that have occasional variations are as follows: Some examples of ʾalif have a slightly flat return rather than a pronounced upward turn. A small number resemble the ʾalif of C.Ia. In most B.II scripts, the tail of the final fā’/qāf curves long way to the right before turning into a hook. Here the tail does not curve so far to the right but drops directly below the round upper part of the letter. In this it is perhaps closer to B.Ib, although the shape of the nib of the calamus is more of a chisel shape and closer to the B.II type. Most examples of final hā’ are closer to B.Ib.

Here the tail does not curve so far to the right but drops directly below the round upper part of the letter. In this it is perhaps closer to B.Ib, although the shape of the nib of the calamus is more of a chisel shape and closer to the B.II type. Most examples of final hā’ are closer to B.Ib.

manuscripts.summary: 397 fol.; Writing surface: parchment; Shelfmark: E. H. 4; Text: Complete Qur'an (?); 15 lines per page; Dimensions: 265 x 210 mm; Script style: kūfī C (see palaeography by Salome Beridze); Estimated date of production: 2nd (8th) to 3rd (9th) centuries [according to Karatay 1962 ].
manuscripts.script: The script style is kūfī type C.III with some variations: The letter ʿayn in the final form resembles the C.II style with the hook wide open. The letter hāʾ is similar to C.I and C.II groups. As for the lām-ʾalif ligature, the two lines curve to each other, sometimes the lām is taller. The diacritical marks are used frequently and are indicated by oblique dashes. The letter qāf is marked with a dot below the letter, which is a common feature in early Qurʾān manuscripts in kūfī script. (Salome Beridze)

manuscripts.title: Tbilisi, Georgian National Museum (საქართველოს ეროვნული მუზეუმი), sxm/ag. 2529
manuscripts.repository: Georgian National Museum (საქართველოს ეროვნული მუზეუმი) , Tbilisi
manuscripts.summary: Single parchment folio, Shelfmark: sxm/ag. 2529; Qurʾānic Text: Q 4:127-135; Lines per page: 13; Dimensions: 345 x 240 mm; Script style: kūfī, variation of the type D.Va; Estimated date of production: 750-850.
manuscripts.script: The script of this Qurʾān fragment represents the kūfī script style D being close to the D.Va group with elements of D.I/D.II as these subgroups are very similar (Déroche 1983: PN 112-116) , (Déroche 1992: PN 44-45) . The letter ʾalif in the independent form has a long lower return with an upward curve. The letter ǧīm in the medial form lies on the base line so that the letter before it is placed a little bit higher. In the final or independent position ǧīm has a tail that curves back to the head of the letter close to the line, similar to the D.I/D.II group. The letter ṭāʾ in the final or independent position is similar to those in the D.Va group. ʿAyn initially resembles the D.Va group. In middle position, the letter shows a vertical right hand antenna which remains vertical in final position as well. Qāf in its final or independent form shows a U-shaped tail that goes backwards as in D.II group. The letter mīm in middle and final position is closer to D.II and D.III group. In both positions the letter lies rather flat on the line, in final position it has a short flat tail. Nūn in final and independent position has a long body and the head is shorter than in D.I, being closer to D.III. Hāʾ in the medial position is placed on the base line, the two eyes are mostly defined. The shape of the letter, which resembles a drop is close to the D.Va group. The lām - ʾalif ligature also resembles D.Va and D.I group, but the lām is much thinner than ʾalif and curves towards it. Unlike Déroche, we use the term kūfī for pragmatic reasons - a term which is attested only after the 10th century. Diacritics: Diacritica of the script are marked by oblique dashes in the same dark brown ink as the main text, mostly, the letters bā ʾ and nūn in final or independent position are marked. Vocalisation is indicated by red and green dots. Some lines show mašq , horizontal stretching of the letters, a technique used to achieve a regular arrangement of letters within a line.

manuscripts.title: Doha, Qatar National Library (مكتبة قطرالوطنية), HC.MS.03177
manuscripts.repository: Qatar National Library (مكتبة قطرالوطنية) , Doha
manuscripts.summary: Four folios: Shelfmarks: HC.MS.03177 , HC.MS.03178 , HC.MS.03179 , HC.MS.03180 Qurʾānic Text: HC.MS.03179r : 006:138-006:144; HC.MS.03179v : 006:144-006:150; HC.MS.03180r : 027:032-027:042; HC.MS.03180v : 027:042-027:055; HC.MS.03177r : 028:033-028:044; HC.MS.03177v : 028:044-028:054: HC.MS.03178r : 028:054-028:064; HC.MS.03178v : 028:064-028:076. Lines per page: 17. Dimensions: HC.MS.03177 : 135 x 182 mm; HC.MS.03178 : 134 x 196 mm; HC.MS.03179 : 135 x 191 mm; HC.MS.03180 : 132 x 200 mm. Script stye: kūfī; ductus: B, with variations. Estimated date of production: 700-900. The present script…
manuscripts.script: Diacritics: Letter-pointing is marked with dots in the shape of the nib of the calamus in the same brown ink as the text. There is no marking of vowels/vocalisation. Discussion of the script: This script is unusual in the variety and inconsistency of letter forms and it does not conform wholly to a specific palaeographic sub-type. Generally most letters are closest to B type scripts, but within the B group the letters vary between B.Ia, B.Ib and B.II, while several letter forms show variations that are closer to A.I or C scripts and there are occasional instances that echo forms seen in ḥiǧāzī scripts. Two particular features are notable: firstly, many examples of the sublinear final and independent forms of lām, nūn and wāw end in a splayed tail that resembles a serif; secondly, many examples of the ʾalif and lām have a distinctive head-serif. Individual diagnostic letter forms in the script found on these leaves and on sister leaves from the same manuscript are as follows: Alif is generally closest to B.II, but the return at the base often turns upwards sharply rather than in a curve. In occasional examples the ascender slopes to the right in a curved form reminiscent of ḥiǧāzī types. As mentioned above, many examples of the ʾalif and lām have an acute head-serif, a feature more often associated with influence from New Style scripts. A comparison for the head-serifs can be found in a script close to D.Vb on a folio in Dār al-Maḫṭūṭāt (Sanaa) DAM 12-29.1 , a script influenced by contemporary non-Qur’ānic scribal practice and showing similarities to "New Style" scripts that François Déroche suggests is associated with the early 10th century ( Déroche 1992, pp. 37-8 ). Interestingly, the splayed, serif-like stub on the sublinear tail of the final lām, nūn and wāw is also found in the same script used on folio Dār al-Maḫṭūṭāt (Sanaa) DAM 12-29.1 . The initial form of ǧīm/ḥā’/ḫāʾ varies between B.Ia, B.II and A.I, while the medial form is closest to B.Ia or B.II. Ṭā’/ẓā’ is closest to B.II. Most examples of initial ‘ayn and ġayn are close to B.II, but some are closer to C.III. Medial ‘ayn is closest to B.II and final ‘ayn is closest to B.Ib. Final fā’/qāf is closest to B.Ia, but some examples are closer to C.III. Forms of the lām-ʾalif vary considerably: some accord with B.Ib or B.II, others are close to A.I, while some resemble C.III. Final mīm is closest to B.Ia, with a pronounced upturned stub. But this upturned stub occasionally extends further upwards into a distinctive short tail, in a form close to one seen in certain ḥiǧāzī scripts, such as those on Ms.orient.Quart.1208(XI) in the Staatsbibliothek Berlin , on Dār al-Maḫṭūṭāt (Sanaa), DAM.01?a , DAM 01-18.3 and Oriental Institute, Chicago, A 7000 . Final nūn is closest to B.Ib and B.II. Hā’ accords closely with B style scripts, but different examples are close to B.Ia, B.Ib and B.II. There is very little use of mašq ("horizontal stretching of the letter forms").

Individual diagnostic letter forms in the script found on these leaves and on sister leaves from the same manuscript are as follows: Alif is generally closest to B.II, but the return at the base often turns upwards sharply rather than in a curve. In occasional examples the ascender slopes to the right in a curved form reminiscent of ḥiǧāzī types. As mentioned above, many examples of the ʾalif and lām have an acute head-serif, a feature more often associated with influence from New Style scripts. A comparison for the head-serifs can be found in a script close to D.Vb on a folio in Dār al-Maḫṭūṭāt (Sanaa) DAM 12-29.1 , a script influenced by contemporary non-Qur’ānic scribal practice and showing similarities to "New Style" scripts that François Déroche suggests is associated with the early 10th century ( Déroche 1992, pp. 37-8 ). Interestingly, the splayed, serif-like stub on the sublinear tail of the final lām, nūn and wāw is also found in the same script used on folio Dār al-Maḫṭūṭāt (Sanaa) DAM 12-29.1 . The initial form of ǧīm/ḥā’/ḫāʾ varies between B.Ia, B.II and A.I, while the medial form is closest to B.Ia or B.II. Ṭā’/ẓā’ is closest to B.II. Most examples of initial ‘ayn and ġayn are close to B.II, but some are closer to C.III. Medial ‘ayn is closest to B.II and final ‘ayn is closest to B.Ib. Final fā’/qāf is closest to B.Ia, but some examples are closer to C.III. Forms of the lām-ʾalif vary considerably: some accord with B.Ib or B.II, others are close to A.I, while some resemble C.III. Final mīm is closest to B.Ia, with a pronounced upturned stub. But this upturned stub occasionally extends further upwards into a distinctive short tail, in a form close to one seen in certain ḥiǧāzī scripts, such as those on Ms.orient.Quart.1208(XI) in the Staatsbibliothek Berlin , on Dār al-Maḫṭūṭāt (Sanaa), DAM.01?a , DAM 01-18.3 and Oriental Institute, Chicago, A 7000 . Final nūn is closest to B.Ib and B.II. Hā’ accords closely with B style scripts, but different examples are close to B.Ia, B.Ib and B.II. There is very little use of mašq ("horizontal stretching of the letter forms").

manuscripts.script: Diacritics: Letter-pointing is marked with dots in the shape of the nib of the calamus in the same brown ink as the text. There is no marking of vowels/vocalisation. Discussion of the script: This script is unusual in the variety and inconsistency of letter forms and it does not conform wholly to a specific palaeographic sub-type. Generally most letters are closest to B type scripts, but within the B group the letters vary between B.Ia, B.Ib and B.II, while several letter forms show variations that are closer to A.I or C scripts and there are occasional instances that echo forms seen in ḥiǧāzī scripts. Two particular features are notable: firstly, many examples of the sublinear final and independent forms of lām, nūn and wāw end in a splayed tail that resembles a serif; secondly, many examples of the ʾalif and lām have a distinctive head-serif. Individual diagnostic letter forms in the script found on these leaves and on sister leaves from the same manuscript are as follows: Alif is generally closest to B.II, but the return at the base often turns upwards sharply rather than in a curve. In occasional examples the ascender slopes to the right in a curved form reminiscent of ḥiǧāzī types. As mentioned above, many examples of the ʾalif and lām have an acute head-serif, a feature more often associated with influence from New Style scripts. A comparison for the head-serifs can be found in a script close to D.Vb on a folio in Dār al-Maḫṭūṭāt (Sanaa) DAM 12-29.1 , a script influenced by contemporary non-Qur’ānic scribal practice and showing similarities to "New Style" scripts that François Déroche suggests is associated with the early 10th century ( Déroche 1992, pp. 37-8 ). Most examples of initial ‘ayn and ġayn are close to B.II, but some are closer to C.III. Medial ‘ayn is closest to B.II and final ‘ayn is closest to B.Ib. Final fā’/qāf is closest to B.Ia, but some examples are closer to C.III. Forms of the lām-ʾalif vary considerably: some accord with B.Ib or B.II, others are close to A.I, while some resemble C.III. Final mīm is closest to B.Ia, with a pronounced upturned stub. But this upturned stub occasionally extends further upwards into a distinctive short tail, in a form close to one seen in certain ḥiǧāzī scripts, such as those on Ms.orient.Quart.1208(XI) in the Staatsbibliothek Berlin , on Dār al-Maḫṭūṭāt (Sanaa), DAM.01?a , DAM 01-18.3 and Oriental Institute, Chicago, A 7000 . Final nūn is closest to B.Ib and B.II. Hā’ accords closely with B style scripts, but different examples are close to B.Ia, B.Ib and B.II. There is very little use of mašq ("horizontal stretching of the letter forms").

manuscripts.title: Rome, Biblioteca Apostolica Vaticana, Vat. Ar. 2063
manuscripts.repository: Biblioteca Apostolica Vaticana , Rome
manuscripts.summary: Shelf mark Vat. Ar. 2063 contains eight fragments of parchment Qurʾāns in different kūfī script styles, which were discovered recently. They belong to the Qurʾān fragments registered under shelf mark Vat. Ar. 1605, described by Georgia Levi Della Vida, cf. for example Vat. Ar. 1605 (1) .

manuscripts.title: Doha, Qatar National Library (مكتبة قطرالوطنية), HC.MS.03113
manuscripts.repository: Qatar National Library (مكتبة قطرالوطنية) , Doha
manuscripts.summary: Single parchment folio; Shelfmark: HC.MS.03113; Qurʾānic Text: Q 6:150-158; Lines per page: 13; Dimensions: 334 x 233, with integrations: 334 x 250; Script style: kūfī, variation of the D.Va typw; Estimated date of production: 750-850.
manuscripts.script: The script of this Qurʾān fragment represents the kūfī script style D being close to the D.Va group with elements of D.I/D.II as these subgroups are very similar (Déroche 1983: PN 112-116) , (Déroche 1992: PN 44-45) . The letter ʾalif in the independent form has a long lower return with an upward curve. The letter ǧīm in the medial form lies on the base line so that the letter before it is placed a little bit higher. In the final or independent position ǧīm has a tail that curves back to the head of the letter close to the line, similar to the D.I/D.II group. The letter ṭāʾ in the final or independent position is similar to those in the D.Va group. ʿAyn initially resembles the D.Va group. In middle position, the letter shows a vertical right hand antenna which remains vertical in final position as well. Qāf in its final or independent form shows a U-shaped tail that goes backwards as in D.II group. The letter mīm in middle and final position is closer to D.II and D.III group. In both positions the letter lies rather flat on the line, in final position it has a short flat tail. Nūn in final and independent position has a long body and the head is shorter than in D.I, being closer to D.III. Hāʾ in the medial position is placed on the base line, the two eyes are mostly defined. The shape of the letter, which resembles a drop is close to the D.Va group. The lām - ʾalif ligature also resembles D.Va and D.I group, but the lām is much thinner than ʾalif and curves towards it. Unlike Déroche, we use the term kūfī for pragmatic reasons - a term which is attested only after the 10th century. Diacritics: Diacritica of the script are marked by oblique dashes in the same dark brown ink as the main text, mostly, the letters bā ʾ and nūn in final or independent position are marked. Vocalisation is indicated by red and green dots. Some lines show mašq , horizontal stretching of the letters, a technique used to achieve a regular arrangement of letters within a line. (Salome Beridze)

manuscripts.title: Istanbul, Topkapı Palace Museum (Topkapı Sarayı Müzesi), H.S. 44/32 [= Karatay 1]
manuscripts.repository: Topkapı Palace Museum (Topkapı Sarayı Müzesi) , Istanbul
manuscripts.summary: 410 fol. + 3 fol.; Writing surface: parchment; ancient Shelfmark: H.S.194, shelfmark today: H.S. 44/32; Text: Q 1-114 (?); 18 lines per page; Dimensions: 460 x 400 mm; Script style: kūfī; Estimated date of production: 1st (7th) to 2nd (8th) centuries according to Karatay 1962 . Edition by Tayyar Altıkulaç, al-Muṣḥāf al-Shari f, 2007.
manuscripts.script: Der Grundtext in schwarzer Tinte ist mit einer dicken Linie geschrieben, wie es in abbasidischer Zeit üblich wurde. Im Vergleich zu späteren Koranhandschriften im kūfī-Duktus ist die Schrift jedoch von einer bemerkenswerten Schlichtheit: Die Linienführung des Schreibers ist in aller Regel gut erkennbar. Die Hasten der Oberlängen ( ʾalif, lām ), die senkrecht zur Grundlinie stehen, sind kürzer als in den älteren Manuskripten und bleiben in der eigenen Zeile. Die Schriftart lässt sich der C-Gruppe (der Typologie nach François Déroche) zuordnen. Diese zeichnet sich dadurch aus, dass (1) die Buchstaben mīm und hāʾ nicht unter die Grundlinie greifen und (2) das nūn einer Klammer (etwa ], aber leicht abgerundete Ecken) ähnelt, welche nach links geöffnet ist. Das späte B II, das dem noch ziemlich nahekommt, greift bei der Schreibung des Buchstaben hāʾ deutlich unter die Grundlinie - was in der vorliegenden Handschrift erkennbar nicht der Fall ist. Innerhalb der C-Gruppe kommt dem Istanbuler Kodex dann der Duktus C I am nächsten, dessen andere Vertreter ins 8. Jahrhundert zu datieren sind (vgl. dazu die Beispiele in F. Déroche, The Abbasid Tradition , S. 36). Zur paläographischen Beschreibung vgl. E. İhsanoǧlu: "An examination of the Topkapı Muṣḥaf shows that it was written with a developed kufic script. The shape of the letters does not confirm to the writing style of the early Muṣḥafs attributed to Caliph ʿUthmān, which were written on vellum in his period and therefore known as the Muṣḥafs of Caliph ʿUthmān. [...] Apparantly this Method of Abū al-Aswad al-Duʾalī [invented after the death of Caliph ʿUthmān] was carefully followed in placing the vowel marks of the Topkapı Palace copy. Single dots were placed in red ink above, beside or below the letters." ( Altıkulaç, al-Muṣḥāf al-Sharif, S. 9). Zur Differenzierung der Homographen dienen auf und unter den Buchstaben dünne Striche, die offenbar mit einem anderen Stift als der Grundtext geschrieben wurden. Für die Vokalisierung wurden rote Punkte verwendet. Es waren also mindestens drei Schreibgeräte bei der Herstellung des Textes beteiligt. Diese Beobachtung wird durch Korrekturen im Text unterstützt: zu Beginn von Q 5:53 wurde zwischen dem Vertrenner und dem ersten Wort nachträglich ein wāw eingefügt (fol. 71 v, Zeile 15). Die Handschrift wird dem Kalifen Osman zugeschrieben, vgl. dazu: "According to the evaluation of Munadjdjid, neither this Muṣḥaf nor the Muṣḥafs located in Tashkent, al-Mashhad al-Ḥusayn in Cairo and in the Museum of Turkish and Islamic Works in Istanbul were the Muṣḥafs attributed to Caliph ʿUthmān; however considering the differences in their script, the centuries when they were copied and their different dimensions, they must have been copied from the Muṣḥafs attributed to Caliph ʿUthmān. For this reason, each of them was called the (Muṣḥaf ʿUthmān)" ( Altıkulaç, al-Muṣḥāf al-Sharif , S. 80). - Altıkulaç dokumentiert einige Stellen, die seiner Meinung nach fehlerhaft zu sein erscheinen: "The Muṣḥaf contains a small number of mistakes which may be attributed to the scribes. - The word كلو, which was written as the last word in folio 5a in Sūrat al-Baqara (2/57), was repeated as the first word of folio 5b; - the word اذا in Sūrat al-Anʿām (6/152) was spelled as اذ.; - the word ولا was written as وولا in Sūrat al-Aʿrāf. (7/192); - the word انى اخاف in Sūrat al-Anfāl (8/48)was spelled with two alifs as اني ااخاف.; - the word صديد was spelled as صدد in Sūrat Ibrāhīm (14/16); - the word لا in the phrase اولاالالبب in Sūrat al-Zumar (39/18) was dropped; - the alif at the end of the word اعدا in Sūrat Fuṣṣilat (41/19) was forgotten; - the phrase يااولى was written as يالاولى and the phrase لانتم was written as لاانتم in Sūrat al-Ḥashr (59/2,13)." ( Altıkulaç, al-Muṣḥāf al-Sharif , S. 75)

manuscripts.title: Istanbul, Topkapı Palace Museum (Topkapı Sarayı Müzesi), "Saray 50386" (Gotthelf Bergsträßer archives) [= Karatay 42]
manuscripts.repository: Topkapı Palace Museum (Topkapı Sarayı Müzesi) , Istanbul
manuscripts.summary: 605 fol., Parchment; Shelfmark: "Saray 50386" (Gotthelf Bergsträßer archives); Qurʾānic Text: Q 2:12- Q 114; Lines per page: 14; Dimensions: 290 x 365 mm (according to Karatay, 1962 ); Script style: kūfī, type F.I (described in François Déroche's Abbasid Tradition , pp.46-47), see Palaeography by Salome Beridze; Estimated date of production: 750-900; Unknown provenance.
manuscripts.script: The script style is kūfī type F.I. The lower return of ʾalif in the independent form is sometimes flatter and longer than in other cases. The letter group ǧīm/ḥāʾ/ḫāʾ shows the same form in the final and isolated position: the tail of the letter has no final loop and lies flat on the base line. This is different in some other F.I manuscripts, where the letter in the final form has a tail that turns back on itself. As for the letter ʿain or ġain , the final form has a circular ending that is open to the right, whereas in the isolated position the letter has a long horizontal tail, as the ǧīm/ḥāʾ/ḫāʾ group. In all other respects, the script is identical to F.I. described in François Déroche's Abbasid Tradition , pp.46-47. (Salome Beridze)

manuscripts.title: Istanbul, Topkapı Palace Museum (Topkapı Sarayı Müzesi), "Saray 50395" (Gotthelf Bergsträßer archives) [= Karatay 40]
manuscripts.repository: Topkapı Palace Museum (Topkapı Sarayı Müzesi) , Istanbul
manuscripts.summary: 363 (?) folios; Parchment; Shelfmark: "Saray 50395" (Gotthelf Bersträßer archives); Complete codex; 16 lines per page; Dimensions: 240 x 330 mm; Script style corresponds to kūfī D IV (typology of F. Déroche 1983, pp. 41-45 with the description of style D) with some variations, see palaeography by Salome Beridze; Estimated date of production: 750-900 (?).
manuscripts.script: The script style of this manuscript is kūfī type D.IV (typology of François Déroche 1983, pp. 41-45 with the description of style D) with some variations. The ʾalif in the independent form has a shorter return at the base and a longer shaft than in D.IV. The letter is closer to the C.Ia style. The ʿain in the initial form has a hook that is larger and more open, which is closer to the D.I and D.III groups. When at the end of a word, the letter form is related to the B.I and B.II groups. The letter qāf in the independent and final forms resembles the D.I. The letters mīm and hāʾ are also related to the B.II group, being close to the D.I style at the same time. As for lām-ʾalif , the form is similar to the C.Ia or C.III groups. The fol. 7 has been replaced and is written in a different (later) script, as well as folio 347 recto and 351 verso. (Salome Beridze)

manuscripts.summary: Der Katalog der Pariser Koranhandschriften (Déroche 1983: p. 71) enthält folgende Angaben: "Arabe 338 c : ffo 36 et 37. Fo 36 à 37 : IV, 95-105. Copie anonyme et non datée. Graphie voisine de B II. Diacritiques : rares traits obliques. Vocalisation : points rouges, partiellement portés. Les versets ne sont pas séparés ; seul un hāʾ doré, qui signale un groupe de cinq versets, est visible. Parchemin. 2 feuillets. Page : 102 x 144. 9 et 10 lignes. Encre brun foncé. Surface d'écriture : 50 x 105."

La sourate XLIII est…

manuscripts.title: Paris, Bibliothèque nationale de France, Arabe 5124
manuscripts.repository: Bibliothèque nationale de France , Paris
manuscripts.summary: Der Katalog der Pariser Koranhandschriften ( Déroche 1983: p. 110-111) enthält folgende Angaben: "Arabe 5124 . Fo 1 à 8 : XLVIII, I-LI, 48. Copie anonyme et non datée. Graphie D IV. Diacritiques : points ajoutés; vocalisation : points rouges. Des rosettes (3.1.2) séparent les versets; des hāʾ couﬁques dorés signalent les groupes de cinq versets, des médaillons de type 2'.A.II ceux de dix. En tête de chaque sourate ﬁgure la formule « fātiḥatu sūrati... » suivie du titre et du nombre des versets a l’encre dorée ; on distingue l’amorce de vignettes (cf. ffo 1 ro, 3 vo, 5 vo et 7 ro). Au fo 8 ro,…

manuscripts.repository: Bibliothèque nationale de France , Paris
manuscripts.summary: Der Katalog der Pariser Koranhandschriften (Déroche 1983: p. 74) enthält folgende Angaben: "Arabe 5178 e : fo 8. Fo 8 : XIV, 48-XV, 14. Copie anonyme et non datée. Graphie B II. Diacritiques : traits obliques ; vocalisation : points rouges complétés par des signes modernes, en vert ; hamza , šadda et sukūn : signes modernes, verts. Des groupes de trois traits obliques (1.1.1) séparent les versets ; des hā’ couﬁques dorés signalent les groupes de cinq versets, des médaillons de type 2’.A.II ceux de dix. En tête de la sourate XV ﬁgurent son titre et le nombre de ses versets, à l’encre dorée…

manuscripts.title: Paris, Bibliothèque nationale de France, Arabe 5178 (j)
manuscripts.repository: Bibliothèque nationale de France , Paris
manuscripts.summary: Der Katalog der Pariser Koranhandschriften (Déroche 1983: p. 96-97) enthält folgende Angaben: "Arabe 5178 j : fo 14 vo : LVIII, 1. Copie anonyme et non datée. Graphie proche de D I. Diacritiques : traits obliques ; vocalisation absente. La sourate est précédée de son titre et du nombre de ses versets, a l’encre dorée dans un bandeau (cf. fo 14 ro). Un décor occupe tout le fo 14 ro. Bandeau (83 X 155) : l’encadrement est réalisé par les entrelacs de deux éléments. Aux quatre coins, des ﬁgures à trois branches dont le contour est indiqué par un ﬁlet doré étendent leurs branches placées sur les…

Copie anonyme et non datée. Graphie proche de D III. Diacritiques : rares traits obliques ; vocalisation : points rouges. Des décors en forme de larme avec le mot aya (4.1.1) séparent les versets; des médaillons de type 3.C.II signalent les groupes de dix versets. Le fo 16 vo présente un bandeau occupant toute la page (56 x 112). Deux boucles constituées d’une chenille de points rouges sur fond blanc, encadrée de ﬁlets dorés,…

manuscripts.summary: Zwei Fragmente der Londoner Khalili-Sammlung ( KFQ 13 und KFQ 14 ) gehören ursprünglich zu demselben Kodex, aus dem auch das Pariser Fragment Arabe 340 (f) stammt. Vom Fragment KFQ 13 liegt uns zur Zeit leider nur ein Bild der verso-Seite vor.

manuscripts.title: London, The Khalili Collections, KFQ 14
manuscripts.repository: The Khalili Collections , London
manuscripts.summary: Das hier angezeigte Fragment KFQ 14 stammt zusammen mit dem Fragment KFQ 13 und einem Fragment aus der Pariser Bibliothèque nationale, Arabe 340 (f) ursprünglich aus demselben Kodex. Leider liegt uns zum Fragment KFQ 14 nur ein Bild der Verso-Seite vor. François Déroche schreibt zum Fragment KFQ 13 (Déroche 1992: p. 54) : "9 / Two folios / 9th century AD / 16x20.3cm, with 16 lines to the page / Material Parchment; the verso is the hair side in both cases / Text area 9.8x15.5cm in KFQ13, and 9.7x15.5cm in KFQ14 / Script Style B.II / Accession nos KFQ13 , KFQ14 / Another fragment from the same…

manuscripts.summary: François Déroche schreibt zum Fragment QUR 48 und QUR 80 (Déroche, The Abbasid Tradition, London 1992 , S. 56): "10 / Qur'an fragment / 9th century AD / 49 folios, 11x17.8cm, with 17 lines to the page / Material Parchment / Text area 8.4x13.9cm / Script Style B.II / Binding Modern / Accession nos QUR48 and QUR80 / The text is written in black ink. manuscripts.title: London, The Khalili Collections, KFQ 55
manuscripts.repository: The Khalili Collections , London
manuscripts.summary: François Déroche schreibt zum Fragment KFQ55 (Déroche, The Abbasid Tradition, London 1992 , S. 71): "23 / Single folio / 9th century AD / Fragmentary (largest dimensions now 16.8x28.5cm), with 15 lines to the page / Material Parchment; the verso is the hair side / Text area 12.5x21.5cm / Script A style related to D.1 / Accession no . KFQ55 / This fragment shows a modified form of D.I: usually a large script, its dimensions have been reduced here, and the different type of pen needed has had an effect on the forms of some letters, particularly nūn . manuscripts.script: The script style of this parchment leave showes features of various script styles described by François Déroche (kūfī script styles, A, B, C, D, E and of New Style). It does not match any types of the script styles of Déroche's typology. Vocalisation is marked by red dots. Green dots and green inserted ʾalifs in green describe a second alternative reading of the text.

manuscripts.title: Doha, Qatar National Library (مكتبة قطرالوطنية), HC.MS.03106
manuscripts.repository: Qatar National Library (مكتبة قطرالوطنية) , Doha
manuscripts.summary: Single folio, Shelfmark: HC.MS.03106, Writing surface: Parchment, Text: 50:2-34, 16 lines per page, Dimensions (of parchment folio, not including cardboard mount): 196 x 289 mm, Script style: kūfī, ductus of writing: C.II and C.Ia, with overlap to C.Ib. Codicology: Recto is flesh side; verso is hair side. Single verse divisions are marked with a small cluster of three nib-shaped diagonal dashes. Fifth verse divisions are marked with a red device in the form of a thick letter ʾalif . Tenth verse divisions are marked with a small red disk. Based on observation of sister folios from the same…
manuscripts.script: Diacritics: Letter-pointing: there are no marks for letter-pointing on the present folio nor on the other published examples. Vocalisation: Red dots, occasional green dots (in many cases the pigment of the green dots has oxidised to brown). Discussion of Script: François Déroche ( 1992, p. 62 ) stated that the script on the folio from this Qur’ān in the Khalili Collection was “close to” C.II, and George ( 2010, p. 153 ) uses the same folio as the exemplar for C.II. Both authors note the close similarity in letter forms between C.Ia, C.Ib and C.II. Close examination of the letter forms on the QNL folio HC.MS.03106 and others from the same Qur’ān reveal that most letter forms vary between C.Ia and C.II. This would accord with Déroche’s comment that C.II may be a variant of C.I. The diagnostic letter forms on the present folio are as follows: ʾalif : most examples of ʾalif lie between C.Ia, and C.II, but some deviate towards C.Ib and to C.III. ǧīm/ḥā’/ḫāʾ : initial ǧīm/ḥā’/ḫā’ is closest to C.Ia. The return of the medial ǧīm/ḥā’/ḫā’ protrudes slightly below the line, a feature more often associated with B scripts, although the present examples have the acute angularity of the C group and sometimes the return is closer to the line in a style close to C.I and C.II. ṭā’/ẓā’ : the tā’/ẓā’ letter-forms are close to C.Ia. ‘ayn/ġayn : both initial and medial ‘ayn/ġayn are closest to C.Ia. qāf : terminal qāf is quite thick and the form lies between C.Ia and C.II. mīm : the form of the terminal mīm lies between C.Ia and C.II, but the tail is shorter (see comment in Déroche 1992, p. 62 ). nūn : terminal nūn exhibits examples close C.Ia, C.II and C.III. hā’ : most examples of hā’ are close to C.II. lām-ʾalif : the form of the lām-ʾalif is closest to C.Ia, the verticals of which curve slightly to the right. There is almost no use of mašq (“horizontal stretching of the letter forms”). Horizontal strokes are sometimes used to fill the end of a line to justify text area ( Déroche 1992, p. 62 ). Orthography: Diacritical dots ( ʾiʿǧām ): there are no diacritical dots on the present folio nor on the other published examples. Vocalisation ( taškīl ): Red dots, occasional green dots (in many cases the pigment of the green dots has oxidised to brown).

manuscripts.title: Doha, Qatar National Library (مكتبة قطرالوطنية), HC.MS.03114
manuscripts.repository: Qatar National Library (مكتبة قطرالوطنية) , Doha
manuscripts.summary: Shelfmark: HC.MS.03114 ; HC.MS.03115 , HC.MS.03116 Text: HC.MS.03115 : 024:061-025:003; HC.MS.03114 : 025:037-025:055; HC.MS.03116 : 025:073-026:022. Lines per page: 17 Dimensions: HC.MS.03115 : 199 x 282 mm; HC.MS.03114 : 199 x 276 mm; HC.MS.03116 : 199 x 275 mm; Script style: kūfī, Ductus: C.III. Estimated date of production: 750-900. Single verse markers consist of clusters of diagonal ink dashes in of type 1.1.1 and 1.1.4; Tenth verse separators consist of two forms. Most consist of a brown ink circle containing a quatrefoil motif in red and green, but on the verso of HC.MS. 03114 , the…
manuscripts.script: Diacritics: Letter-pointing consists of angled dashes in the same dark brown ink as the main script. Vocalisation consists of red and green dots. Discussion of script: Generally the script is closest to C.III. There are two letter forms that are unusual: Final qāf has an elongated tail that hangs through the next line and ends between the one and two lines below. One example of a final nūn has a very eccentric form. In line 13 of the recto of 03116, final nūn of the final word of 26:4 has a tail that curves round in a complete circle. It is very different to all the other examples of final nūn on these three folios and is an extremely rare form in general. There is some use of mašq (“horizontal stretching of the letter forms”).

manuscripts.title: Istanbul, Süleymaniye Library (Süleymaniye Kütüphanesi), Ayasofya 23
manuscripts.repository: Süleymaniye Library (Süleymaniye Kütüphanesi) , Istanbul
manuscripts.summary: Die Schwarzweißbilder einer Handschrift in kufischer Schrift (D.I) wurden um 1930 aus Istanbul aufgenommen und an Gotthelf Bergsträßer in München verschickt (siehe Bild 2). Es handelt sich um 114 entwickelte Fotos, von denen keine Negativfilme vorliegen. Der Schriftduktus lässt sich in der Typologie von François Déroche als D.I klassifizieren. Die Herkunft der Handschrift zu der keine Angaben in den Listen des Bergsträßer-Archivs vorhanden sind, bleibt unbekannt.

manuscripts.title: Paris, Bibliothèque nationale de France, Arabe 381 (a)
manuscripts.repository: Bibliothèque nationale de France , Paris
manuscripts.summary: Der Katalog der Pariser Koranhandschriften (Déroche 1983: p. 130) enthält folgende Angaben: "Arabe 381 a : ffo 1 à 54. Fo 1 à 36 : IX. 112-X, 6; fo 37 à 46 : X, 88-XI, 7; fo 47 à 54; LXIV, 7-LXV, 6. Copie anonyme et non datée. Graphie du groupe D. Diacritiques absents, sauf quelques ajouts en couleur ; vocalisation : points rouges. Les versets ne sont pas séparés ; des hāʾ couﬁques dorés signalent les groupes de cinq versets, des cercles de type 1.B.I ceux de dix. Les sourates sont précédées de leur titre et du nombre de leurs versets à l'encre dorée (cf. ffo 9 vo, 44 vo et 51 ro). Parchemin.…

manuscripts.title: Cambridge, Cambridge University Library, MS Add.1116
manuscripts.repository: Cambridge University Library , Cambridge
manuscripts.summary: 37 folios; Writing surface: parchment; Shelfmark: Add.1116; Qurʾānic text: Q 3:62-89; 3 lines per page; Dimensions: 189 x 123mm; Script style: kūfī D I; Estimated date of production: Before 262 AH/875–76 CE (dedication by Amaǧūr, Abbasid governor of Damascus 870-878 CE).

manuscripts.title: Paris, Bibliothèque nationale de France, Arabe 339
manuscripts.repository: Bibliothèque nationale de France , Paris
manuscripts.summary: Der Katalog der Pariser Koranhandschriften (Déroche 1983: p. 85) enthält folgende Angaben: "Arabe 339 : Fo 1 : II, 101-113; fo 2 à 76 : II, 191-VIII, 7. Copie anonyme et non datée. Graphie D I. Diacritiques : traits obliques, presque au complet ; vocalisation : points rouges ; hamza : points verts. Les versets ne sont pas séparés ; un hāʾ doré indique les groupes de cinq versets ; des médaillons de type 2’.A.II à l'intérieur desquels est inscrit une lettre-chiffre marquent les groupes de dix versets. Chaque sourate est précédée d’un bandeau à l’intérieur duquel sont indiqués son titre et le…

manuscripts.script: "Lower portion of a larger page. 4 lines ; recto is hair ; early Abbasid script. Red dots ; mark for end of each verse; mark for five verses on the margin, consisting of inscription in gold inscribed in a circle in gold, with black and red dots. Support eaten by ink."

manuscripts.script: "Dense writing surface with 16 lines to the page ; early Abbasid script (yāʾ 6 mm.). The flesh/hair sequence is as follows: FHFHHFHF. Red dots. Stained."

manuscripts.title: Paris, Bibliothèque nationale de France, Arabe 372
manuscripts.repository: Bibliothèque nationale de France , Paris
manuscripts.summary: The text has no seperators after individual verses, but after 5 (e.g., on fol. 1v in line 5) and ten verses (e.g., on fol. 25r in line 5 after the word al-qahhār an illuminated sign marks the end of ten verses; or in fol. 22v , line 4). The number of verses of a surah is registered in the surah headings (fol. 26r , fol. 43v and fol. 55v ). Vocalisation is given by red dots, occasionally green dots appear, the function of which is not clear, e.g., on fol. 26r (line 3), fol. 8r (line 1), fol. 7v (line 6), fol. 8v (line 2), fol. 9r (line 5), fol. 10v (lines 3 and 4), fol. 17v (lines 4 and 5),…

manuscripts.title: Paris, Bibliothèque nationale de France, Arabe 379 (c)
manuscripts.repository: Bibliothèque nationale de France , Paris
manuscripts.summary: Der Katalog der Pariser Koranhandschriften ( Déroche 1983 : p. 115) enthält folgende Angaben: "Arabe 379c : ffo 31 à 39. Fo 31 à 32 : II, 273-282; fo 33 à 34 : V, 119-VI, 12; fo 35 à 36 : XLIII, 80-XLIV, 18; fo 37 à 39 : XLV. 23-XLVI, 7. Copie anonyme et non datée. Graphie a rapprocher de NS II. Diacritiques : points ; vocalisation : points rouges. Les versets ne sont pas séparés ; des hāʾ couﬁques signalent les groupes de cinq versets, des médaillons de type 2.A.II avec le nom de la dizaine en toutes lettres ceux de dix. En tête des sourates, dans un bandeau, ﬁgurent le titre et le nombre…

manuscripts.title: London, The Khalili Collections, KFQ 78
manuscripts.repository: The Khalili Collections , London
manuscripts.summary: Cf. entry by François Déroche about fragment KFQ 78 (Déroche, The Abbasid Tradition, London 1992 , p. 123): "67 / Single folio / 9th century AD / 11.2x20.3cm / Material Parchment / Accession no . KFQ78 / Published Sotheby’s, London, 14 December 1987, lot no. 149 / The folio bears no text, for both sides are occupied by a panel of illumination characteristic of the Abbasid period. In contrast to cat.75, where the central field occupies a smaller proportion of the design as a whole, the development of the central pattern is here given more prominence. On one side, the illumination (6.8x14.4cm)…

manuscripts.title: Los Angeles, California, Los Angeles County Museum of Art, M.2002.1.352

manuscripts.title: Cleveland, Ohio, The Cleveland Museum of Art, 1933.483
manuscripts.repository: The Cleveland Museum of Art , Cleveland, Ohio
manuscripts.summary: Single folio parchment of a Qurʾān; Dimensions: 174 x 130 mm; 14 lines per page; Qurʾānic text: Q 9:30-37; Script style: kūfī B.II; Estimated date of production: 800-900 CE. The text on the fol. 1r is partly illegible. The main text is written in dark brown ink. Traces of gold ink can be seen. One sister folio has been identified from the same collection with the shelfmark 1933.482 .
manuscripts.script: Script style is kūfī B.II. The hook of the letter ʿayn in the initial form is extended. The horizontal tail of the letter mīm in the final or isolated position is also extended. The tail of the letter qāf in the final position is vertical compared to the typical shape of the style B.II, where the ending of the letter goes to the right. Diacritical marks are missing. The vocalisation is done by red dots.

manuscripts.title: Doha, Qatar National Library (مكتبة قطرالوطنية), HC.MS.03167
manuscripts.repository: Qatar National Library (مكتبة قطرالوطنية) , Doha
manuscripts.summary: Four folios from the same Qur’ān: Shelfmark: HC.MS.2017.0009 , HC.MS.03119 , HC.MS.03167 and HC.MS.2017.0010 . Qurʾānic Text: HC.MS.2017.0009 : 011:116-012:006; HC.MS.03119 : 026:061-026:093; HC.MS.03167 : 034:050-035:008; HC.MS.2017.0010 : 046:010-046:020. Dimensions: HC.MS.2017.0009 : 193 x 260 mm; HC.MS.03119: 170 x 219 mm; HC. MS.03167: 194 x 256 mm; HC.MS.2017.0010 : 193 x 250 mm. Recent provenance of these folios/published: HC.MS.03119 : Christie’s, London, Islamic Art, 27 April 1993, lot 18; HC.MS.03167 : Sotheby’s auction, London, 27 April 1994, lot 5 (dimensions were given as 19.2 x…
manuscripts.script: Some use of mašq (“horizontal stretching of the letter forms”). On some folios the lines of script slant downwards or upwards slightly. Letter-pointing is very rarely used, and consists of diagonal nib-shaped dashes. Vocalisation consists of red dots. Script style: kūfī, Ductus of writing: Main Qur’anic text: B.II, Incidentals: D.I; François Déroche suggested that the script style B.II was in use by the early ninth century ( Déroche 1992, p. 36 ), while Alain George suggests that it may have begun to develop as early as the first half of the eighth century ( George 2010, p. 91 ). However, George also notes that the use of D group script in the gold sura headings in some Qur’ans in B.II indicate that it was still used in the ninth century, D group scripts being typical of ninth-century Abbasid manuscripts. The manuscript from which the present folios originate has sura headings in D.I script (e.g. HC.MS.2017.0009 verso and HC.MS.03167 recto ), indicating that this particular manuscript was produced in the ninth century. Verse markers: Single verse divisions are marked with a small cluster of two or three nib-shaped diagonal dashes (identical shape to the letter points). Tenth verse divisions are marked with a small roundel with concentric rings of brown and red ink surrounding a gold interior. Sura heading: Sura headings are written in gold ink in script D.I. The gold ink is written over the top of red ink. The red ink may represent an earlier writing of the sura heading or may have been used as a colour base to give the gold a richer hue.

manuscripts.title: Washington DC, Library of Congress, AL-16
manuscripts.repository: Library of Congress , Washington DC
manuscripts.summary: Single folio on parchment in horizontal format; Qurʾanic text: Q 6:68-75; Sixteen lines per page; AL-16 and another fragment from the same collection AL-11 and two other fragments from the Freer Gallery collection F1945.19 and F1945.20 originally belonged to the same codex. For a detailed description of sister fragments of these fragments see the entry by Marcus Fraser: "Doha, Qatar National Library: HC.MS.2017.0010 ".
manuscripts.script: Script: kūfī type B.II. The letter ʾalif is always vertical, with short return at the baseline in the independent form. The characteristic shape of the letter qāf in the final position is given with its ending going far to the right. The mašq -method, the horizontal elongation of the letters, is used in the manuscript. Vocalisation is made by red dots. Diacritical marks are used sparsely, originally only the letter nūn in the independent position is being marked with a dash.

manuscripts.title: Washington DC, Library of Congress, AL-11
manuscripts.repository: Library of Congress , Washington DC
manuscripts.summary: Single folio on parchment in horizontal format; Qurʾanic text: Q 27:14-27; 16 lines per page; AL-11 and another fragment from the same collection AL-16 and three other fragments, two from the Freer Gallery collection F1945.19 and F1945.20 and one from Qatar National Library HC.MS.2017.0010 originally belonged to the same codex. For a detailed description of the sister fragments see the entry by Marcus Fraser HC.MS.2017.0010 .

manuscripts.title: Cambridge, Michaelides Collection, No. 190
manuscripts.repository: Michaelides Collection , Cambridge
manuscripts.summary: This papyrus was once part of the Georges Michaélidès collection, but is no longer traceable in the collection now held by the University of Cambridge. An illustration of the lost fragment can be found in an essay by Adolf Grohmann (cf. Grohmann 1961 ). Neither the date of origin nor the location are known. It is one of the few Qur'anic fragments on papyrus, although it is unclear whether it was part of a codex. The surah transitions are marked by the basmala and - in line 8 of fol. 2r and in line 15 of fol. 1v - by simple ornaments. It seems that verses are not noted. The writer usually…

Au fo 11 r, une note dans une écriture très soignée rappelle que ce volume a été constitué waqf dans la grande mosquée de Tarse par al-Ḥasan b. Ḥamdān serviteur ( ġūlām ) de Sākin al-Kīġliġī ; une autre main a ajouté en-dessous en écriture cursive la date de l’acte, 300 /913-914, et le nom de l’intermédiaire, al-Ḥaǧǧāǧ b. Sindī b. ʿAlī ; enﬁn, au-dessus de la première note, une troisième main a indiqué, en écriture cursive…

Les versets ne sont pas séparés ; des hāʾ couﬁques dorés marquent les groupes de cinq versets, des médaillons de type 2.C.I ceux de dix. En tête de la sourate XIX ﬁgurent le titre et le nombre des versets a l’encre dorée (cf. fo 31 vo). Parchemin.…

manuscripts.title: Doha, Qatar National Library (مكتبة قطرالوطنية), HC.MS.2017.0015
manuscripts.repository: Qatar National Library (مكتبة قطرالوطنية) , Doha
manuscripts.summary: Two folios; Shelfmark: HC.MS.03223 ; HC.MS.2017.0015 ; Qurʾānic Text: HC.MS.03223 : 003:001-003:008, HC.MS.2017.0015 : 003:017-003:024; Lines per page: 15; Dimensions: HC.MS.03223 : 287 x 368 mm; HC.MS.2017.0015 : 289 x 378 mm; Script stye: kūfī; Ductus: D group; Estimated date of production: 9th-10th century. Single verse separators: the original separators consist of short diagonal row of angled gold dashes, of type 1.2.1 ( Déroche 1983, p. 27 ). At a later date the original gold dashes have been over-painted with silver discs. Fifth verse separators: the original separators consist of…
manuscripts.script: Diacritics: Letter-pointing consists of small gold dashes of the same shape as the original inter-versal markers, but occurs very rarely and inconsistently. Many pages have no letter-pointing at all, whereas some have occasional instances that do not seem to follow any pattern of usage. For example, there is no use of letter-pointing on either of the folios in QNL, but on the folio in the Museum of Fine Arts, Boston (33.686), letter pointing is used on a tā’ in the 12th line and another tā’ in the 14th line; and on the folio in the Museum of Islamic Art, Doha (Ms.8.2006) a ṯā’ on line 7 of the recto is pointed with three clearly visible diagonal gold dashes. There is no vocalisation. Discussion of the script: The difficulty of applying gold script onto a page of parchment means that the shapes of the letter forms of this script are less clearly defined than for calligraphic scripts written with a calamus and standard brown inks. The technique of chrysography involves the mixing of powdered gold with an adhesive substance such as gum Arabic to make a gold “ink” whose slightly viscous and grainy nature makes it more difficult to apply. Another method involved writing the script with an adhesive liquid (size, glair, gum Arabic) and then applying powdered gold ( Déroche et al. 2015, p. 118 ). In both cases the gold script was then outlined with a thin line in a contrasting colour. Perhaps as a result of the challenges of these techniques, the gold script on the Blue Qur’an has a greater number of variations in letter forms and the categorisation of the ductus is not straightforward. In many cases different examples of the same letters relate to more than one of the sub-types of the D scripts. Françōis Déroche ascribed it to D.IV but noted several letter forms that are atypical of D.IV or even unique to the Blue Qur’an ( 1992, p. 43, cats. 42, pp. 92-3 ) and Alain George follows this categorisation, pointing out certain letter forms that he suggests are archaic within the D group as well as a related variation in the form of the word Allah ( George 2009, pp. 80-81 ). He associated these forms with influence from styles B.II and C.III and suggested that the script of the Blue Qur’an was a transitional script, being an early expression of D scripts retaining some influence from B and C groups, and he attributed manuscript to the period c. 750-850, a period when he suggests D.IV was developing. However, observation of the present two leaves as well as many others in the known corpus reveals many variations in the letter forms, the great majority of which relate to different sub-groups within the D group of scripts, with a small number relating to other script types. This means that it is difficult to precisely define the script of the Blue Qur’an as any specific sub-group. Thus it is more sensible to define the script of the Blue Qur’an as falling generally in the D group rather than being one specific sub-type of the D group, or perhaps being a hybrid script with a foundation in D scripts. There is frequent use of mašq (“horizontal stretching of the letter forms”). A selection of the variations of individual letter forms based on a survey of many pages of the Blue Qur’ān is as follows: Different examples of initial ʾalif are close to D.I, D.II, D.III and D.IV. The lower return most often rests on the line rather than curving upwards. In this it is closest to D.III, but the end is less gradually tapering and often ends in an angled tip. In most cases the ascenders of lām-alif diverge widely, in a form close to D.I and D.II, and in some instances the form resembles D.Va, and occasionally B.II, but is not similar to D.IV. Initial ǧīm/ḥā’/ḫā’ is close to D.I, D.II and D.IV and occasionally C.III, while the medial form is close to D.I. Final form is close to D.III. Initial ‘ayn and ġayn has a more angular return than most D script examples, and its exact form lies between C.III and D.Vb. Different examples of medial ‘ayn and ġayn are close to D.I, D.II, D.III and D.IV. Déroche states that the final form is a D.IV type, but an atypical shape ( Déroche 1992, p. 43 ). Final fā’/qāf is close to D.I and D.IV. The form of the sublinear hook has a shorter or non-existent ligature joining the upper circle to the lower hook in the present script, meaning the lower hook rests against the upper circle. Final mīm sometimes has a tail that is quite thick and straight, at other times it is a small stub that tapers to a point, and occasionally it widens to a small wedge shape. These forms most often relate to different D types, and more rarely to F. Overall most examples are closest to D.I and D.III. Final hā’ is at different times close to D.I, D.II and D.IV and D.Va. Medial hā’ is sometimes close to D.Vb and even occasionally to E. George points out that examples of the word Allah have the group of lām-lām-hā’ arranged so that the heads of each letter descend to the left in a straight diagonal line, the cluster creating a triangular form ( George 2009, p. 81 ). He links this to influence from C.II and C.III. However, close examination shows that many examples of the word Allah do not have this form, rather, the two letters lām are almost the same height, but the hā ’ is significantly shorter. Final nūn is closest to D.IV.

manuscripts.title: Cairo, Khedivial Library, today: Egyptian National Library and Archives (دار الكتب والوثائق القومية), Moritz 1905, table 45 (below)
manuscripts.repository: Khedivial Library, today: Egyptian National Library and Archives (دار الكتب والوثائق القومية) , Cairo
manuscripts.summary: Der Gesamtumfang des wahrscheinlich aus Ägypten stammenden Fragments ist unbekannt. Bernhard Moritz ordnet das Fragment ins 3./4. Jahrhundert ein (Moritz 1905: Tafel 45 unten) . Ein ähnliches Koranfragment mit der Signatur E 16265 wird in Philadelphia aufbewahrt und wurde von Giorgio Levi Della Vida beschrieben, der eine Datierung ins 5./6. islamische Jahrhundert vorschlägt.

Fo 1 à 2: II, 137-141; fo 3 à 10 : III, 169-186; fo 11 à 18 : IV, 51-68 ; fo 19 : V, 76-78 ; fo 20 : XVII, 20-22; fo 21 : XVII, 47-49; fo 22 : XVIII, 42-45; fo 23 : XVIII, 57-58. manuscripts.title: London, The Khalili Collections, KFQ 9
manuscripts.repository: The Khalili Collections , London
manuscripts.summary: Cf. entry by François Déroche about fragment KFQ 45 (Déroche, The Abbasid Tradition, London 1992 , p. 129): „72 / Single folio / End of the 9th century AD or 10th / 11 x 17 cm, with 8 lines to the page / Material Parchment; the recto is the hair side / Text area 5.5 x 10.9 cm / Script Style D.V, with some influence of E / Accession no . KFQ 9 / The text, which consists of Sūrat ṭāʾhāʾ (XX), verses 15-24, is written in black ink, with red dots to indicate the vocalization. Verses are not divided. A I.B.I device in gold, red and green marks the end of a group of ten verses at XX, 21. The upper…

manuscripts.title: London, The Khalili Collections, KFQ 48
manuscripts.repository: The Khalili Collections , London
manuscripts.summary: Cf. catalogue entry by F. Déroche about this fragment ( The Abbasid Tradition, London 1992 , p. 138f.): "75 / Three folios / First half of the 10th sentury AD / 13.5x20 cm, with 6 lines to the page / Material Parchment; the recto is the hair side in folio 1, while the verso is the hair side in folios 2 and 3 / Text area 6.7x14 cm / Script Style NS.III / Accession no. KFQ48 / Another fragment from the same Qurʾan Paris, Bibliothèque Nationale, MS.arab.382c ( Déroche 1983 , no.259) / This fragment provides a fine example of the NS.III script. As with cat.79, it is possible to draw a parallel…

manuscripts.title: Doha, Qatar National Library (مكتبة قطرالوطنية), HC.MS.2017.0053
manuscripts.repository: Qatar National Library (مكتبة قطرالوطنية) , Doha
manuscripts.summary: Single folio, Shelfmark: HC.MS.2017.0053, Writing surface: parchment, Text: 34:45-48, Lines per page: 5, Dimensions: 148 x 210 mm, Script style: kūfī, gold Ductus: D.I/D.II and D.Vb; Estimated date of production: 10th century. Provenance: Medieval provenance: Two folios bear medieval waqf inscriptions indicating that parts of the manuscript were in the Great Mosque of Qayrawan in medieval times (see http://islamicart.museumwnf.org/database_item.php?id=object;ISL;tn;Mus01;3;en&pageD=N ; Déroche 1992, p. 67 ; Sotheby’s, London, 12 October 2005, lot 6). However, the folios in the Bibliothèque…
manuscripts.script: Diacritics: Letter-pointing consists of diagonal dashes in brown ink. Vocalisation consists of red, green and blue dots. The script is generally close to D.I, but there are two forms of the lām-ʾalif . One is the conventional form associated with D.I, in which the ascenders diverge (verso, fourth line); while the other has ascenders that initially diverge and then curve inwards again at the top (recto, final letter of the fifth line). The latter form is associated with D.Vb and New Style scripts, which were in use in the tenth century. These variations are also noted on other folios from this Qur’ān, as well as instances of D.Vc letter forms ( Stanley 1995, p.18 ). François Déroche notes that in addition to the precisely applied dashes of the letter-pointing, the scribe has included minute letters ‘ayn and rā’ to differentiate from their homographs ( Déroche 1992, no. 19, p. 67 ). There are no examples of these marks on the present folio, but there are two instances in which the letter ḥā’ is differentiated from its homographs, the letters ǧīm and ḫāʾ, by the use of a distinctive angled mark ( > ) below the letter. Thus this Qur’ān features a very precise diacritical system. As with other early examples of Qur’ānic chrysography, the script is either written in a gold “ink” in which powdered gold is mixed with a binder, or first written with a size or glair and gold powder is then applied to this, leaving gold letters that follow the form of the adhesive script beneath. The gold is then outlined in brown ink to give it visual and graphic definition. There is very limited use of mašq (“horizontal stretching of the letter forms”).

manuscripts.title: Doha, Qatar National Library (مكتبة قطرالوطنية), HC.MS.03101
manuscripts.repository: Qatar National Library (مكتبة قطرالوطنية) , Doha
manuscripts.summary: Single folio, Shelfmark: HC.MS.03101, Writing surface: parchment, Text: 18:1-21, Lines per page: 13, Dimensions: 178 x 255 mm; with integrations 227 x 293. Sister leaves: There are no sister leaves currently known from this Qur’ān.
manuscripts.script: Script style: kūfī, Ductus: Main Qur’ānic Text: D.Vc with elements of D.Vb; Incidentals (gold sura heading of Sura 018): D.I/D.II; Diacritics: Letter-pointing ( ʾiʿǧām ): diagonal dashes in brown ink (original); Vocalisation ( taškīl ): red and green dots. Estimated date of production: 10th century; François Déroche notes cautiously that a script close to D.Vc is used in a waqf inscription dated 298/911 ( Déroche 1992, p. 42 ), and Fraser notes that certain letter forms are closer to New Style scripts ( Fraser and Kwiatkowski 2006, pp. 52-57 , where further discussion of this script can be found). Both authors suggest a tenth-century origin for Qur’āns written in this script. In the main Qur’ānic text, all letters are D.Vc except the tā’/ẓā’ . There are two forms of the letter tā’/ẓā’ . In one, the ascender rises vertically, as in D.Vc, whereas in the other the ascender rises at an oblique angle and curves over at the top, which is the form of tā’/ẓā’ used in D.Vb. The overlapping nature of these two ductae is shown by other shared features. For example, other manuscripts written in D.Vc employ two forms of the final nūn , one the angular form found in D.Vb and the other the rounded form of D.Vc (see ( Fraser and Kwiatkowski 2006, p. 52, fig. 5, p. 53 main illustration ). The oblique curved form of tā’/ẓā’ found on the present folio is also frequently found in New Style scripts of the tenth century and later (e.g., inter alia, a copy of the Mawaqif dated 344/956, see George 2010, p. 126, fig. 73 ; the "Muṣḥaf al-Hāḍinah Qur’ān" ("Qurʾān of the Nurse"), made at Qayrawan in 1019-20, see Fraser and Kwiatkowski 2006, p. 60 ), and is thus an indication of a transitional aspect in the script D.Vc. The same applies to the inward-curving verticals of the lām-ʾalif , which are found in kūfī D.Vb (e.g. the Isfahan Qur’ān of 383/993, see Déroche 1992, no.83, pp. 154-5 , although not on the present leaf). It therefore seems that D.Vb and D.Vc represent a style and a period when the influence of New Style scripts was beginning to impact on the established kūfī styles. This would in turn accord with the probability that this script was in use in the tenth century. There is no use of mašq (“horizontal stretching of the letter forms”) and the length of each letter is very regular.

manuscripts.title: Paris, Bibliothèque nationale de France, Arabe 382 (a)
manuscripts.repository: Bibliothèque nationale de France , Paris
manuscripts.summary: 17 folios; Shelfmark: Arabe 382 (a); Writing surface: parchment; Qurʾānic text: ; Lines per page: 6, Script style: New Style (type III); Dimensions: 189 x 138 mm. Estimated date of production: 1000-1200; according to the catalogue of François Déroche (Déroche 1983 : p.141): "Arabe 382 a : ffo 1 à 17. Fo 1 à 4: IV, 115-137; fo 5 : VIII, 4—10;fo 6: IX, 95-100 ; fo 7 à 8 : X, 1-11;fo 9 à 12 : XVIII, 34-60; fo 13 à 14 : LX, 3-10; fo 15 à 17 : LXI, 6-LXII, 7. Copie anonyme et non datée. Graphie NS III. Diacritiques absents; vocalisation : points rouges. Les versets ne sont pas séparés ; des hāʾ…
